message.content: acquaint

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches ŌĆö when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness ŌĆö fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly ŌĆö if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; highŌåÆmax: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only ŌĆö it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 ŌåÆ 4.6, 4.6 ŌåÆ 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

file.content:
# Todo

This file contains near term tasks with a short description
and reference links to more details.

Intro paragraphs in `## Todo` and `## Bugs` should begin every
line with 1 leading space so they don't match the `^\d+\. `
pattern that locates numbered entries; 2 or 3 spaces also work.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" ŌĆö a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

_No cycle currently in progress._

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** ŌĆö #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs ŌĆö to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](/AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. **merge-non-ff recipe: codify in `notes/cycle-protocol.md`.**
   Multi-commit cycles default to the merge-non-ff trapezoid,
   but the recipe is split between `cycle-protocol.md` and
   AGENTS.md with wrinkles re-derived each time (exercised
   manually on `0.62.0`). Codify the full recipe and
   standardize the flag spelling. Split out of Idea #1 (the
   broader cycle-protocol.md codification).
   - Recipe: `jj rebase -r <closeout> --onto <prev-closeout>
     --onto <work-tip>` (first `--onto` = trunk ŌåÆ first
     parent), then `jj new <merge>` to lift `@` above the
     merge, then `jj git push --bookmark main`.
   - Standardize on `--onto` / `-o` (canonical), not the
     `-d` alias ŌĆö update AGENTS.md's `-d` spellings (jj
     Basics + the post-amend `jj new` note) to `--onto`,
     plus a one-line "`-d` is an alias of `--onto`" so the
     docs don't read as two different flags.
   - `@` reverts to the work-tip's content until the
     `jj new` (the "post-amend `jj new`" gotcha already in
     AGENTS.md).
   - Post-hoc caveat: if the cycle was already pushed
     keep-separate (as `0.62.0` was), the rebase needs
     `--ignore-immutable` and the push is a force-update of
     `main`; the standard recipe assumes the merge is set up
     before the close-out push.
2. **validate-numbering: rename the pair, check all

64	   `todo.md`'s `## Ideas` section passes unnoticed ŌĆö too weak

66	   validators (Todo "pre-commit: single rule ...").

68	     `fix-todo` ŌåÆ `fix-numbering` ŌĆö they validate numbered-
69	     sequence integrity, not todos specifically.

75	   - Default scope: a fixed list of sequence-managed notes

content.content:
1	# Cycle protocol
2	
3	## Cycles
4	
5	A cycle has three phases:
6	
7	- **[Preparation](#preparation)** (`X.Y.Z-0`) ŌĆö the cycle's
8	  first commit. Sets up the cycle:
9	  - Backfill the previous cycle's chores `Commits:` ref.
10	  - Bump `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
11	  - Pick up a `## Todo` item (typically the top-ranked,
12	    #1) into `## In Progress` (bold title + succinct problem
13	    statement + plan ladder).
14	  - Open the [chores section](#chores-sections).
15	- **[Work-N](#work-n)** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, ŌĆ”) ŌĆö the
16	  commits that implement the change. As many as the change
17	  needs; each runs through the
18	  [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
19	- **[Close-out](#close-out)** (bare `X.Y.Z`) ŌĆö the cycle's
20	  last commit. Bookkeeping only:
21	  - Move the picked-up item to a one-line entry in
22	    `## Done`.
23	  - Move the `## In Progress` block into the
24	    [chores section](#chores-sections).
25	  - Optionally update `notes/README.md` if functionality
26	    changed.
27	
28	A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
29	either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the
30	result must always be published at close-out. See
31	[Pushing](#pushing).
32	
33	**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
34	warrant its own ladder, it subdivides ŌĆö `X.Y.Z-3.0`
35	Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
36	Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
37	at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
38	suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
39	for the local-ladder mechanics.
40	
41	## Chores sections
42	
43	A **chores section** is a `##` section in
44	`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording one cycle:
45	
46	- One section per cycle is the default.
47	- Per-commit sections (like 0.55.0's four) are a
48	  deliberate alternative.
49	
50	The phrase **"Open" the chores section** means append a
51	`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
52	with the cycle's anticipated close-out title (e.g.
53	`## refactor: foo bar (X.Y.Z)`). The body is empty;
54	content arrives at close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out)).
55	
56	Fuller chores conventions (content rules, header sync,
57	design subsection pattern, `Commits:` formatting) live in
58	AGENTS.md [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions).
59	
60	## Preparation
61	
62	The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):
63	
64	- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores section
65	  `Commits:` ref** ŌĆö see
66	  [Chores commit references](../AGENTS.md#chores-commit-references).
67	- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
68	- **Pick up a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one).
69	  Move into `## In Progress` as:
70	  - A **bold title line** ŌĆö exact match of the chores
71	    section header, minus the `## ` prefix.
72	  - A **succinct problem statement**.
73	  - A **plan ladder**.
74	
75	  See [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). A Todo that
76	  duplicates current `## In Progress` work is a process
77	  bug.
78	- **Open the [chores section](#chores-sections)** ŌĆö
79	  append a `##` header with the cycle's anticipated
80	  close-out title.
81	
82	## Work-N
83	
84	The cycle's work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, ŌĆ”)
85	implement the change. As many as needed:
86	
87	- Each commit runs through the
88	  **[per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow)**.
89	- **Interim pushes** are optional (backup, progress
90	  visibility).
91	- Close-out is the only mandatory push (see
92	  [Pushing](#pushing)).
93	- **Subdivide into a sub-cycle** if a Work commit's
94	  scope grows enough (see
95	  [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)).
96	
97	## Close-out
98	
99	The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) does bookkeeping
100	only ŌĆö the commit body describes that bookkeeping, not
101	what happens post-squash:
102	
103	- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
104	  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
105	- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the
106	  [chores section](#chores-sections)** (the title-only
107	  placeholder opened during Preparation):
108	  - Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
109	    `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line ŌĆö the
110	    chores `##` header already carries that string).
111	  - Paste under the chores `##` header.
112	  - Sync the chores header to the **final** commit title
113	    if the cycle's scope shifted; update every anchor
114	    back-reference.
115	  - Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
116	    commits; optional `### Outcome` notes.
117	  - Replace `## In Progress` with
118	    `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
119	- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
120	  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).
121	
122	Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
123	publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time ŌĆö
124	see [Pushing](#pushing).
125	
126	## Numbering
127	
128	The version suffix on each commit encodes its phase ŌĆö
129	the **final identifier `0` marks a Preparation**:
130	
131	- `X.Y.Z-0` ŌĆö Preparation
132	- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, ŌĆ” ŌĆö Work commits
133	- `X.Y.Z` ŌĆö Close-out (bare version, no suffix)
134	
135	Disambiguation:
136	
137	- `-10` ŌĆö Work commit #10 (final identifier `10`), not a
138	  Preparation.
139	- `-1.0` ŌĆö Preparation of the `-1` sub-cycle (final
140	  identifier `0`).
141	
142	**Nesting.** Sub-cycles append another level, recursively:
143	
144	- `X.Y.Z-3.0` ŌĆö Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle
145	- `X.Y.Z-3.1`, `X.Y.Z-3.2` ŌĆö its Work
146	- `X.Y.Z-3` ŌĆö its Close-out
147	- `X.Y.Z-3.1.0` ŌĆö Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle
148	
149	Sub-cycles needing no Preparation omit `.0` (`-3.1`,
150	`-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later adds
151	`-3.0` without renumbering siblings.
152	
153	Bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase so `vc-x1 -V`
154	reports the active phase at build time.
155	
156	## Per-commit flow
157	
158	Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
159	through:
160	
161	1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
162	   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`.
163	2. **Do the work** (see [Iterative work](#iterative-work)
164	   for the loop-and-squash technique).
165	3. **Flip this commit `(current)` ŌåÆ `(done)`** in `## In
166	   Progress` ŌĆö before the cargo cycle and the commit.
167	4. **Cargo cycle** (skip-able for purely-docs commits; full
168	   cycle mandatory at close-out):
169	   1. `cargo fmt`
170	   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
171	   3. `cargo test`
172	   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
173	   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
174	5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
175	   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
176	   changes and we iterate until complete.
177	6. **Write the commit description** ŌĆö see
178	   [Commit description](#commit-description).
179	7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
180	   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
181	8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
182	   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
183	   keeps the verb visible):
184	
185	   ```
186	   jj commit -m \
187	   "<type>: <short description> (<version>)" \
188	   -m "<intro paragraph>
189	
190	   - file1: gist
191	   - file2: gist
192	
193	   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
194	   -R .
195	   ```
196	
197	**Two overrides apply:**
198	
199	- **Deviation or question** ŌĆö any time the work deviates
200	  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
201	  surface it; don't push through.
202	- **ESC-ESC** ŌĆö the user can interrupt at any point to pull
203	  a review or question forward.
204	
205	## Commit description
206	
207	[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/)
208	with a version suffix:
209	
210	```
211	<type>: <short description> (<version>)
212	```
213	
214	### Title
215	
216	- Ōēż50 chars total (version suffix counts).
217	- Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`,
218	  `docs`, `chore`.
219	- Favor terse phrasings.
220	
221	### Body
222	
223	[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form) (intro + bullets),
224	wrap Ōēż72. Bullet content differs per repo:
225	
226	- **App-repo body**: file-by-file. One bullet per file
227	  changed (file plus a one-line gist). Sub-bullets for
228	  files with multiple distinct changes:
229	
230	  ```
231	  - path/to/file1
232	    - first distinct change
233	    - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
234	       with continuation indented 5
235	  ```
236	
237	  The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
238	  cycle's mechanical change record. Chores carries the
239	  narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
240	  "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###` subsection.
241	
242	- **Session-repo (`.claude`) body**: bullets describe
243	  in-session activity rather than code changes.
244	
245	### Trailer
246	
247	`ochid:` as the last line of the body ŌĆö see
248	[ochid trailers](#ochid-trailers).
249	
250	## Reviewing changes
251	
252	Work review looks at the **uncommitted working-copy diff**,
253	on the way to commit. The user opens diffs in their
254	editor (Zed, VSCode); jj commands are for terminal:
255	
256	- `jj diff` ŌĆö working-copy diff (uncommitted)
257	- `jj diff -r @-` ŌĆö diff of the previous commit
258	- `jj diff --from <X> --to <Y>` ŌĆö any two revisions
259	- `jj show -r <X>` ŌĆö description + diff for one rev
260	
261	Don't `jj edit -r @-` to view a past commit ŌĆö that marks
262	it mutable and shifts `@`; use `jj diff -r @-` or
263	`jj show -r @-`.
264	
265	See [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders) for the
266	close-out squash recipe and recovery; revset primitives
267	are in [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md).
268	
269	## Pushing
270	
271	### Policy
272	
273	Push is **discretionary** during the cycle (backup,
274	progress visibility) and **mandatory at close-out** ŌĆö
275	the cycle's result must be published.
276	
277	### Shape at close-out push
278	
279	Decide the shape and get user approval before pushing ŌĆö
280	the choice is hard to undo once on the target. Surface
281	the options, wait for the user.
282	
283	- **Squash to one commit** ŌĆö single entry on the target.
284	  Right for straightforward changes where the Work-N is
285	  focused on one or two files.
286	- **Merge non-ff** ŌĆö `main` gains a merge commit
287	  (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via two
288	  parents. `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` shows the trapezoidal
289	  shape. Set up: `jj rebase -r <tip> --onto <prev>
290	  --onto <sub-tip>` where `<prev>` is the previous
291	  cycle's closed-out commit (current main tip). Two
292	  `--onto` ŌåÆ `<tip>` becomes a merge of `<prev>` +
293	  `<sub-tip>`.
294	- **Keep separate** ŌĆö one commit per cycle entry on
295	  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
296	  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
297	  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.
298	
299	Set up squash/merge before invoking `vc-x1 push`; use
300	`jj git push` directly for non-standard shapes.
301	
302	### vc-x1 push wrapper
303	
304	`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps per-push mechanics. See
305	`vc-x1 push --help` for current flags.
306	
307	**Current limitation**: only fully supports the
308	[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) shape; other
309	shapes need manual jj steps. Improvements tracked /
310	planned:
311	
312	- N:1 codeŌåöbot for Merge non-ff (`## Todo` entry P1).
313	- Symmetric squash (planned, to be captured in `-2`).
314	- Per-repo bookmark names (planned).
315	
316	### .claude cadence
317	
318	**Cadence**: one push = one `.claude` commit, paired
319	with every code commit in that push.
320	
321	The `.claude` working copy accumulates session data
322	across the cycle; its change ID stays stable across
323	snapshots, `jj describe`, and the finalize commit, so
324	code-side `ochid:` trailers resolve.
325	
326	`.claude` is a linear journal ŌĆö all session work lives
327	on `main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
328	create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror
329	app-side branches** ŌĆö risks the bot steering session
330	pushes to the wrong remote ref.
331	
332	### Bot communication at the reviews
333	
334	Use plain prose ŌĆö no insider jargon ("Gate N signal",
335	"Checkpoint N", etc.):
336	
337	- **At Work review** ŌĆö summarize what changed and stop.
338	  "Work complete. Please review."
339	- **At Commit Description review** ŌĆö present `$TITLE`
340	  and `$BODY` explicitly; ask permission to commit/push.
341	  Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push ... --title ...
342	  --body ...` invocation by default.
343	- **At Post close-out review** ŌĆö surface the shape
344	  options (squash / merge / keep) and the push target;
345	  wait for the user's choice before any `jj squash` /
346	  `jj rebase` / `jj git push` invocation.
347	
348	### After push or finalize: stop and wait
349	
350	After you **push** (cross the remote boundary) or launch
351	**finalize** on the `.claude` repo (`vc-x1 finalize --repo
352	.claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach`) ŌĆö by
353	hand or via the `vc-x1 push` wrapper ŌĆö you **MUST NEVER**
354	proceed (next step, edit, tool call, text output) until the
355	user explicitly directs you to continue. **Even when the next
356	step seems obvious ŌĆö wait.** Treat push-or-finalize as a hard
357	stop for the whole turn.
358	
359	`vc-x1 push` performs both as its tail stages ŌĆö `push-app`
360	then the detached `vc-x1 finalize` on `.claude` ŌĆö so there is
361	no gap left to speak in once the push is invoked. Put **all**
362	closing words *before* invoking it; do not purposely emit anything
363	after the tool returns.
364	
365	Why (`.claude` is the live journal finalize snapshots ŌåÆ
366	squashes ŌåÆ pushes):
367	
368	- **`--detach`**: the bot always emits information after a tool
369	  returns so by having the tool detach the bot may perform the
370	  additional work.
371	- **`--delay 10`**: gives the bot the time it needs to do
372	  the additional work after the tool has detached and thus
373	  the tool can include it in the commit.
374	- **silence**: Reinforces that the bot must not summarize or
375	  perform any unnecessary work after the tool returns.
376	
377	### Recovery
378	
379	- **If push exits before `finalize-claude`** (e.g.
380	  failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run
381	  finalize by hand:
382	
383	  ```
384	  vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
385	  ```
386	
387	- **Clear push's saved state** after any out-of-band
388	  recovery ŌĆö `rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml` or `vc-x1 push
389	  <bookmark> --restart` ŌĆö otherwise push resumes from a
390	  stale stage.
391	- **Late code-repo tweak after `push-app` succeeded**
392	  (e.g. updating AGENTS.md or memory) requires `jj
393	  squash --ignore-immutable` and a re-push; that is a
394	  remote rewrite and needs explicit approval like any
395	  push.
396	
397	## ochid trailers
398	
399	A **chid** is jj's change ID ŌĆö a permanent identifier
400	that survives rebases and describes (see
401	[`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md)). An **ochid** trailer
402	on a commit body links it to its counterpart in the
403	other repo via that counterpart's chid, written as a
404	workspace-root-relative path. See
405	[`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) for the
406	multi-line trailer design (multi-user / forking).
407	
408	- **Code-side commits** each carry one
409	  `ochid: /.claude/<.claude-chid>` ŌĆö the `.claude`
410	  change ID.
411	- **The `.claude` commit** has one `ochid: /<code-chid>`
412	  per code commit in that push. More than one occurs on
413	  Merge non-ff close-out (one ochid per Work commit in
414	  the cycle).
415	
416	Use `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L` to capture the change
417	IDs (first line app, second `.claude`).
418	
419	## Iterative work
420	
421	When work for a single commit (the **target**) benefits
422	from incremental review, loop:
423	
424	1. `jj new -R .` ŌĆö fresh empty `@` on top of the target.
425	2. Make the next round of changes.
426	3. User reviews the round (see
427	   [Reviewing changes](#reviewing-changes)).
428	4. `jj squash -R .` folds into the target and creates a
429	   new empty `@`.
430	5. If not done, go to step 2.
431	
432	Same jj mechanics as a
433	[sub-cycle ladder](#sub-cycle-ladders), but at
434	single-commit scope ŌĆö the cycle's version suffix
435	doesn't change.
436	
437	## Sub-cycle ladders
438	
439	When a Work commit subdivides into a sub-cycle (see
440	[Numbering](#numbering) for suffix nesting), its Work
441	commits typically live as a local jj `@` chain and
442	**collapse into the sub-cycle's Close-out** before the
443	parent cycle continues. Ladder commits are scratch ŌĆö
444	for review and bisection only.
445	
446	### Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder
447	
448	For each Work commit in the ladder:
449	
450	1. `jj new -R .` ŌĆö create a fresh empty `@`.
451	2. Do the commit's work.
452	3. Run `cargo test --bins`. **Non-negotiable** ŌĆö build
453	   and clippy alone miss regressions until a later
454	   commit runs the full suite, raising bisection cost.
455	4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` ŌĆö working title
456	   only (no version suffix); the sub-cycle Close-out
457	   collects everything into one final commit with the
458	   `(X.Y.Z-N)` marker.
459	
460	### Navigating the ladder
461	
462	Common moves:
463	
464	- `jj log -r '<base>::' -R .` ŌĆö see the whole ladder
465	  from its base.
466	- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` ŌĆö jump `@` to any ladder
467	  commit by chid prefix; useful for bisection.
468	- `jj edit @-- -R .` ŌĆö quick-jump back two commits.
469	- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` ŌĆö review one commit in
470	  isolation.
471	
472	Modifications to any ladder commit rewrite it in place;
473	descendants auto-rebase.
474	
475	### Close-out: squash the ladder
476	
477	When all ladder Work commits are done and tests pass:
478	
479	```
480	jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
481	```
482	
483	`<base>` is the parent of the first ladder commit; `-u`
484	keeps `@`'s description and discards the sources'.
485	After squash, history is linear: `<base> ŌåÆ @`;
486	intermediate commits are auto-abandoned.
487	
488	Then `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` as for any other commit.
489	
490	For N = 1 the squash is a no-op (`<base>..@-` is empty
491	when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit directly.
492	
493	### Recovery
494	
495	If a ladder commit goes wrong, back out without losing
496	prior commits:
497	
498	- **Discard the current commit.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
499	  drops it; you get a fresh empty `@` on the same
500	  parent.
501	- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
502	  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
503	  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
504	- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows
505	  the op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts
506	  to that point. Full undo ŌĆö removes *all* ladder work
507	  after the chosen op. Use only to start over.
508	
509	# References
510	
511	- [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md) ŌĆö revset primitives
512	  (chid/cid, `@`/`@-`/`@+`, `..`/`::` ranges, prefix matching).
513	- [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) ŌĆö multi-ochid
514	  trailer design (fork / multi-user generalization).
515	- [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh) ŌĆö script that
516	  scaffolds a 4-revision ladder under `/tmp/substep-test`
517	  for squash-recipe experiments.
518	- 0.41.1-6.5 cycle ŌĆö first multi-commit ladder usage. The
519	  per-commit `cargo test --bins` gate originated there
520	  after a regression introduced in an early commit wasn't
521	  caught until a later one ran the full suite.
522

file.content:
# Cycle protocol

## Cycles

A cycle has three phases:

- **[Preparation](#preparation)** (`X.Y.Z-0`) ŌĆö the cycle's
  first commit. Sets up the cycle:
  - Backfill the previous cycle's chores `Commits:` ref.
  - Bump `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
  - Pick up a `## Todo` item (typically the top-ranked,
    #1) into `## In Progress` (bold title + succinct problem
    statement + plan ladder).
  - Open the [chores section](#chores-sections).
- **[Work-N](#work-n)** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, ŌĆ”) ŌĆö the
  commits that implement the change. As many as the change
  needs; each runs through the
  [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
- **[Close-out](#close-out)** (bare `X.Y.Z`) ŌĆö the cycle's
  last commit. Bookkeeping only:
  - Move the picked-up item to a one-line entry in
    `## Done`.
  - Move the `## In Progress` block into the
    [chores section](#chores-sections).
  - Optionally update `notes/README.md` if functionality
    changed.

A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the
result must always be published at close-out. See
[Pushing](#pushing).

**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
warrant its own ladder, it subdivides ŌĆö `X.Y.Z-3.0`
Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
for the local-ladder mechanics.

## Chores sections

A **chores section** is a `##` section in
`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording one cycle:

- One section per cycle is the default.
- Per-commit sections (like 0.55.0's four) are a
  deliberate alternative.

The phrase **"Open" the chores section** means append a
`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
with the cycle's anticipated close-out title (e.g.
`## refactor: foo bar (X.Y.Z)`). The body is empty;
content arrives at close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out)).

Fuller chores conventions (content rules, header sync,
design subsection pattern, `Commits:` formatting) live in
AGENTS.md [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions).

## Preparation

The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):

- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores section
  `Commits:` ref** ŌĆö see
  [Chores commit references](../AGENTS.md#chores-commit-references).
- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
- **Pick up a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one).
  Move into `## In Progress` as:
  - A **bold title line** ŌĆö exact match of the chores
    section header, minus the `## ` prefix.
  - A **succinct problem statement**.
  - A **plan ladder**.

  See [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). A Todo that
  duplicates current `## In Progress` work is a process
  bug.
- **Open the [chores section](#chores-sections)** ŌĆö
  append a `##` header with the cycle's anticipated
  close-out title.

## Work-N

The cycle's work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, ŌĆ”)
implement the change. As many as needed:

- Each commit runs through the
  **[per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow)**.
- **Interim pushes** are optional (backup, progress
  visibility).
- Close-out is the only mandatory push (see
  [Pushing](#pushing)).
- **Subdivide into a sub-cycle** if a Work commit's
  scope grows enough (see
  [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)).

## Close-out

The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) does bookkeeping
only ŌĆö the commit body describes that bookkeeping, not
what happens post-squash:

- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the
  [chores section](#chores-sections)** (the title-only
  placeholder opened during Preparation):
  - Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
    `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line ŌĆö the
    chores `##` header already carries that string).
  - Paste under the chores `##` header.
  - Sync the chores header to the **final** commit title
    if the cycle's scope shifted; update every anchor
    back-reference.
  - Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
    commits; optional `### Outcome` notes.
  - Replace `## In Progress` with
    `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).

Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time ŌĆö
see [Pushing](#pushing).

## Numbering

The version suffix on each commit encodes its phase ŌĆö
the **final identifier `0` marks a Preparation**:

- `X.Y.Z-0` ŌĆö Preparation
- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, ŌĆ” ŌĆö Work commits
- `X.Y.Z` ŌĆö Close-out (bare version, no suffix)

Disambiguation:

- `-10` ŌĆö Work commit #10 (final identifier `10`), not a
  Preparation.
- `-1.0` ŌĆö Preparation of the `-1` sub-cycle (final
  identifier `0`).

**Nesting.** Sub-cycles append another level, recursively:

- `X.Y.Z-3.0` ŌĆö Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle
- `X.Y.Z-3.1`, `X.Y.Z-3.2` ŌĆö its Work
- `X.Y.Z-3` ŌĆö its Close-out
- `X.Y.Z-3.1.0` ŌĆö Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle

Sub-cycles needing no Preparation omit `.0` (`-3.1`,
`-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later adds
`-3.0` without renumbering siblings.

Bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase so `vc-x1 -V`
reports the active phase at build time.

## Per-commit flow

Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
through:

1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`.
2. **Do the work** (see [Iterative work](#iterative-work)
   for the loop-and-squash technique).
3. **Flip this commit `(current)` ŌåÆ `(done)`** in `## In
   Progress` ŌĆö before the cargo cycle and the commit.
4. **Cargo cycle** (skip-able for purely-docs commits; full
   cycle mandatory at close-out):
   1. `cargo fmt`
   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
   3. `cargo test`
   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
   changes and we iterate until complete.
6. **Write the commit description** ŌĆö see
   [Commit description](#commit-description).
7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
   keeps the verb visible):

   ```
   jj commit -m \
   "<type>: <short description> (<version>)" \
   -m "<intro paragraph>

   - file1: gist
   - file2: gist

   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
   -R .
   ```

**Two overrides apply:**

- **Deviation or question** ŌĆö any time the work deviates
  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
  surface it; don't push through.
- **ESC-ESC** ŌĆö the user can interrupt at any point to pull
  a review or question forward.

## Commit description

[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/)
with a version suffix:

```
<type>: <short description> (<version>)
```

### Title

- Ōēż50 chars total (version suffix counts).
- Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`,
  `docs`, `chore`.
- Favor terse phrasings.

### Body

[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form) (intro + bullets),
wrap Ōēż72. Bullet content differs per repo:

- **App-repo body**: file-by-file. One bullet per file
  changed (file plus a one-line gist). Sub-bullets for
  files with multiple distinct changes:

  ```
  - path/to/file1
    - first distinct change
    - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
       with continuation indented 5
  ```

  The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
  cycle's mechanical change record. Chores carries the
  narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
  "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###` subsection.

- **Session-repo (`.claude`) body**: bullets describe
  in-session activity rather than code changes.

### Trailer

`ochid:` as the last line of the body ŌĆö see
[ochid trailers](#ochid-trailers).

## Reviewing changes

Work review looks at the **uncommitted working-copy diff**,
on the way to commit. The user opens diffs in their
editor (Zed, VSCode); jj commands are for terminal:

- `jj diff` ŌĆö working-copy diff (uncommitted)
- `jj diff -r @-` ŌĆö diff of the previous commit
- `jj diff --from <X> --to <Y>` ŌĆö any two revisions
- `jj show -r <X>` ŌĆö description + diff for one rev

Don't `jj edit -r @-` to view a past commit ŌĆö that marks
it mutable and shifts `@`; use `jj diff -r @-` or
`jj show -r @-`.

See [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders) for the
close-out squash recipe and recovery; revset primitives
are in [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md).

## Pushing

### Policy

Push is **discretionary** during the cycle (backup,
progress visibility) and **mandatory at close-out** ŌĆö
the cycle's result must be published.

### Shape at close-out push

Decide the shape and get user approval before pushing ŌĆö
the choice is hard to undo once on the target. Surface
the options, wait for the user.

- **Squash to one commit** ŌĆö single entry on the target.
  Right for straightforward changes where the Work-N is
  focused on one or two files.
- **Merge non-ff** ŌĆö `main` gains a merge commit
  (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via two
  parents. `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` shows the trapezoidal
  shape. Set up: `jj rebase -r <tip> --onto <prev>
  --onto <sub-tip>` where `<prev>` is the previous
  cycle's closed-out commit (current main tip). Two
  `--onto` ŌåÆ `<tip>` becomes a merge of `<prev>` +
  `<sub-tip>`.
- **Keep separate** ŌĆö one commit per cycle entry on
  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.

Set up squash/merge before invoking `vc-x1 push`; use
`jj git push` directly for non-standard shapes.

### vc-x1 push wrapper

`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps per-push mechanics. See
`vc-x1 push --help` for current flags.

**Current limitation**: only fully supports the
[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) shape; other
shapes need manual jj steps. Improvements tracked /
planned:

- N:1 codeŌåöbot for Merge non-ff (`## Todo` entry P1).
- Symmetric squash (planned, to be captured in `-2`).
- Per-repo bookmark names (planned).

### .claude cadence

**Cadence**: one push = one `.claude` commit, paired
with every code commit in that push.

The `.claude` working copy accumulates session data
across the cycle; its change ID stays stable across
snapshots, `jj describe`, and the finalize commit, so
code-side `ochid:` trailers resolve.

`.claude` is a linear journal ŌĆö all session work lives
on `main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror
app-side branches** ŌĆö risks the bot steering session
pushes to the wrong remote ref.

### Bot communication at the reviews

Use plain prose ŌĆö no insider jargon ("Gate N signal",
"Checkpoint N", etc.):

- **At Work review** ŌĆö summarize what changed and stop.
  "Work complete. Please review."
- **At Commit Description review** ŌĆö present `$TITLE`
  and `$BODY` explicitly; ask permission to commit/push.
  Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push ... --title ...
  --body ...` invocation by default.
- **At Post close-out review** ŌĆö surface the shape
  options (squash / merge / keep) and the push target;
  wait for the user's choice before any `jj squash` /
  `jj rebase` / `jj git push` invocation.

### After push or finalize: stop and wait

After you **push** (cross the remote boundary) or launch
**finalize** on the `.claude` repo (`vc-x1 finalize --repo
.claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach`) ŌĆö by
hand or via the `vc-x1 push` wrapper ŌĆö you **MUST NEVER**
proceed (next step, edit, tool call, text output) until the
user explicitly directs you to continue. **Even when the next
step seems obvious ŌĆö wait.** Treat push-or-finalize as a hard
stop for the whole turn.

`vc-x1 push` performs both as its tail stages ŌĆö `push-app`
then the detached `vc-x1 finalize` on `.claude` ŌĆö so there is
no gap left to speak in once the push is invoked. Put **all**
closing words *before* invoking it; do not purposely emit anything
after the tool returns.

Why (`.claude` is the live journal finalize snapshots ŌåÆ
squashes ŌåÆ pushes):

- **`--detach`**: the bot always emits information after a tool
  returns so by having the tool detach the bot may perform the
  additional work.
- **`--delay 10`**: gives the bot the time it needs to do
  the additional work after the tool has detached and thus
  the tool can include it in the commit.
- **silence**: Reinforces that the bot must not summarize or
  perform any unnecessary work after the tool returns.

### Recovery

- **If push exits before `finalize-claude`** (e.g.
  failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run
  finalize by hand:

  ```
  vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
  ```

- **Clear push's saved state** after any out-of-band
  recovery ŌĆö `rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml` or `vc-x1 push
  <bookmark> --restart` ŌĆö otherwise push resumes from a
  stale stage.
- **Late code-repo tweak after `push-app` succeeded**
  (e.g. updating AGENTS.md or memory) requires `jj
  squash --ignore-immutable` and a re-push; that is a
  remote rewrite and needs explicit approval like any
  push.

## ochid trailers

A **chid** is jj's change ID ŌĆö a permanent identifier
that survives rebases and describes (see
[`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md)). An **ochid** trailer
on a commit body links it to its counterpart in the
other repo via that counterpart's chid, written as a
workspace-root-relative path. See
[`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) for the
multi-line trailer design (multi-user / forking).

- **Code-side commits** each carry one
  `ochid: /.claude/<.claude-chid>` ŌĆö the `.claude`
  change ID.
- **The `.claude` commit** has one `ochid: /<code-chid>`
  per code commit in that push. More than one occurs on
  Merge non-ff close-out (one ochid per Work commit in
  the cycle).

Use `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L` to capture the change
IDs (first line app, second `.claude`).

## Iterative work

When work for a single commit (the **target**) benefits
from incremental review, loop:

1. `jj new -R .` ŌĆö fresh empty `@` on top of the target.
2. Make the next round of changes.
3. User reviews the round (see
   [Reviewing changes](#reviewing-changes)).
4. `jj squash -R .` folds into the target and creates a
   new empty `@`.
5. If not done, go to step 2.

Same jj mechanics as a
[sub-cycle ladder](#sub-cycle-ladders), but at
single-commit scope ŌĆö the cycle's version suffix
doesn't change.

## Sub-cycle ladders

When a Work commit subdivides into a sub-cycle (see
[Numbering](#numbering) for suffix nesting), its Work
commits typically live as a local jj `@` chain and
**collapse into the sub-cycle's Close-out** before the
parent cycle continues. Ladder commits are scratch ŌĆö
for review and bisection only.

### Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder

For each Work commit in the ladder:

1. `jj new -R .` ŌĆö create a fresh empty `@`.
2. Do the commit's work.
3. Run `cargo test --bins`. **Non-negotiable** ŌĆö build
   and clippy alone miss regressions until a later
   commit runs the full suite, raising bisection cost.
4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` ŌĆö working title
   only (no version suffix); the sub-cycle Close-out
   collects everything into one final commit with the
   `(X.Y.Z-N)` marker.

### Navigating the ladder

Common moves:

- `jj log -r '<base>::' -R .` ŌĆö see the whole ladder
  from its base.
- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` ŌĆö jump `@` to any ladder
  commit by chid prefix; useful for bisection.
- `jj edit @-- -R .` ŌĆö quick-jump back two commits.
- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` ŌĆö review one commit in
  isolation.

Modifications to any ladder commit rewrite it in place;
descendants auto-rebase.

### Close-out: squash the ladder

When all ladder Work commits are done and tests pass:

```
jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

`<base>` is the parent of the first ladder commit; `-u`
keeps `@`'s description and discards the sources'.
After squash, history is linear: `<base> ŌåÆ @`;
intermediate commits are auto-abandoned.

Then `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` as for any other commit.

For N = 1 the squash is a no-op (`<base>..@-` is empty
when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit directly.

### Recovery

If a ladder commit goes wrong, back out without losing
prior commits:

- **Discard the current commit.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
  drops it; you get a fresh empty `@` on the same
  parent.
- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows
  the op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts
  to that point. Full undo ŌĆö removes *all* ladder work
  after the chosen op. Use only to start over.

# References

- [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md) ŌĆö revset primitives
  (chid/cid, `@`/`@-`/`@+`, `..`/`::` ranges, prefix matching).
- [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) ŌĆö multi-ochid
  trailer design (fork / multi-user generalization).
- [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh) ŌĆö script that
  scaffolds a 4-revision ladder under `/tmp/substep-test`
  for squash-recipe experiments.
- 0.41.1-6.5 cycle ŌĆö first multi-commit ladder usage. The
  per-commit `cargo test --bins` gate originated there
  after a regression introduced in an early commit wasn't
  caught until a later one ran the full suite.

## docs: tighten after-finalize rule (0.63.1)

The stop-and-wait rule in `cycle-protocol.md` was titled
`### After finalize: stop and wait` and phrased as "final
words go in the approval prompt *before* executing finalize"
ŌĆö wording that assumes finalize is a separate,
manually-invoked step and names only finalize as the
trigger. With `vc-x1 push` the push and `vc-x1 finalize` on
`.claude` are bundled as the tail stages, so there is no
"before finalize" gap left to speak in once the push is
invoked. After `0.63.0`'s push the bot emitted a closing
summary *after* `vc-x1 push` returned, violating the rule

  are named, in the title and the paragraph: the remote
  crossing is the push itself, and `vc-x1 finalize` on
  `.claude` is the detached step. This also covers a manual
  `jj git push` (push, no finalize) and a manual
  `vc-x1 finalize` (no wrapper).
- **Wrapper case spelled out** ŌĆö `vc-x1 push` does both as
  tail stages (`push-app`, then `vc-x1 finalize` on
  `.claude`): closing words go *before* invoking the wrapper,
  and the bot does not purposely emit anything after it
  returns. The rule names the `vc-x1 finalize` operation, not
  the wrapper's internal `finalize-claude` stage label.

The bot thinks the original lapse was a compliance slip (the
rule was read and quoted, then broken), not a comprehension
gap; the doc change closes the one genuine ambiguity (the
wrapper bundles push + finalize, leaving no gap to speak in)
rather than expecting prose alone to prevent the slip. The
rule lives only in `cycle-protocol.md`; AGENTS.md already
mandates reading it before commit work, so no duplicate
restatement was added there.

# Chores-13

Continuation of `chores-12.md` (closed at `0.62.x`). This
file covers the `0.63.0` cycle onward.

Subsection headers that record a release use the
trailing-version format from AGENTS.md: `## Description (X.Y.Z)`.
Reference numbering is file-local ŌĆö see
[`AGENTS.md`](../../AGENTS.md#reference-numbering); chores-13
starts at `[1]`.

## docs: adopt AGENTS.md (0.63.0)

Commits: [[1]]

The bot-instructions file is renamed `CLAUDE.md` ŌåÆ `AGENTS.md`
so Zed and the broader agent-tooling ecosystem (which now
default to `AGENTS.md`) read it natively. Claude Code still
auto-loads only `CLAUDE.md`, so a one-line `CLAUDE.md` import
shim (`@AGENTS.md`) keeps it working ŌĆö both tools read one
canonical file.

- **Single canonical file** ŌĆö `AGENTS.md` holds the content;
  `CLAUDE.md` is a one-line `@AGENTS.md` import for Claude
  Code's loader. Nothing human-facing points at the shim.
- **Live references repointed** ŌĆö every `CLAUDE.md` mention
  and `#anchor` link in live docs (`README.md`,
  `ARCHITECTURE.md`, `notes/`, the `src/push.rs` pre-commit
  doc comment, and `AGENTS.md` itself) now names `AGENTS.md`,
  so links resolve in editors and on GitHub.
- **History left as written** ŌĆö `CLAUDE.md` prose in
  `chores-01..12` and `done.md` records what was true at the
  time and is untouched; only the live navigational anchor
  links in the `chores-10/11/12` headers
  (`#reference-numbering`) were repointed so they don't
  dangle against the one-line `CLAUDE.md` shim.

### Why an import shim, not a symlink

A symlink `CLAUDE.md ŌåÆ AGENTS.md` also satisfies Claude
Code's loader, but the `@AGENTS.md` import was chosen
because it is explicit and visible in a diff and does not
depend on symlink semantics (git symlink handling,
cross-platform checkout). Separately, GitHub renders a
symlinked `.md` as a stub page rather than the target's
markdown, so a `CLAUDE.md#anchor` link through a symlink
would not resolve ŌĆö moot here since live links now point
straight at `AGENTS.md`, but it reinforces keeping
`CLAUDE.md` a real file.

## docs: tighten after-finalize rule (0.63.1)

The stop-and-wait rule in `cycle-protocol.md` was titled

- **Wrapper case spelled out** ŌĆö `vc-x1 push` does both as

  and the bot does not purposely emit anything after it
  returns. The rule names the `vc-x1 finalize` operation, not
  the wrapper's internal `finalize-claude` stage label.

The bot thinks the original lapse was a compliance slip (the
rule was read and quoted, then broken), not a comprehension
gap; the doc change closes the one genuine ambiguity (the
wrapper bundles push + finalize, leaving no gap to speak in)
rather than expecting prose alone to prevent the slip. The
rule lives only in `cycle-protocol.md`; AGENTS.md already

input.old_string:
1. **merge-non-ff recipe: codify in `notes/cycle-protocol.md`.**
   Multi-commit cycles default to the merge-non-ff trapezoid,
   but the recipe is split between `cycle-protocol.md` and
   AGENTS.md with wrinkles re-derived each time (exercised
   manually on `0.62.0`). Codify the full recipe and
   standardize the flag spelling. Split out of Idea #1 (the
   broader cycle-protocol.md codification).
   - Recipe: `jj rebase -r <closeout> --onto <prev-closeout>
     --onto <work-tip>` (first `--onto` = trunk ŌåÆ first
     parent), then `jj new <merge>` to lift `@` above the
     merge, then `jj git push --bookmark main`.
   - Standardize on `--onto` / `-o` (canonical), not the
     `-d` alias ŌĆö update AGENTS.md's `-d` spellings (jj
     Basics + the post-amend `jj new` note) to `--onto`,
     plus a one-line "`-d` is an alias of `--onto`" so the
     docs don't read as two different flags.
   - `@` reverts to the work-tip's content until the
     `jj new` (the "post-amend `jj new`" gotcha already in
     AGENTS.md).
   - Post-hoc caveat: if the cycle was already pushed
     keep-separate (as `0.62.0` was), the rebase needs
     `--ignore-immutable` and the push is a force-update of
     `main`; the standard recipe assumes the merge is set up
     before the close-out push.
2. **validate-numbering: rename the pair, check all

   AGENTS.md with wrinkles re-derived each time (exercised
   manually on `0.62.0`). Codify the full recipe and
   standardize the flag spelling. Split out of Idea #1 (the
   broader cycle-protocol.md codification).
   - Recipe: `jj rebase -r <closeout> --onto <prev-closeout>
     --onto <work-tip>` (first `--onto` = trunk ŌåÆ first
     parent), then `jj new <merge>` to lift `@` above the
     merge, then `jj git push --bookmark main`.
   - Standardize on `--onto` / `-o` (canonical), not the
     `-d` alias ŌĆö update AGENTS.md's `-d` spellings (jj
     Basics + the post-amend `jj new` note) to `--onto`,
     plus a one-line "`-d` is an alias of `--onto`" so the
     docs don't read as two different flags.
   - `@` reverts to the work-tip's content until the
     `jj new` (the "post-amend `jj new`" gotcha already in
     AGENTS.md).
   - Post-hoc caveat: if the cycle was already pushed
     keep-separate (as `0.62.0` was), the rebase needs
     `--ignore-immutable` and the push is a force-update of
     `main`; the standard recipe assumes the merge is set up
     before the close-out push.
2. **validate-numbering: rename the pair, check all

## docs: codify merge-non-ff recipe (0.64.0)

# References
toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Chores-13

Continuation of `chores-12.md` (closed at `0.62.x`). This
file covers the `0.63.0` cycle onward.

Subsection headers that record a release use the
trailing-version format from AGENTS.md: `## Description (X.Y.Z)`.
Reference numbering is file-local ŌĆö see
[`AGENTS.md`](../../AGENTS.md#reference-numbering); chores-13
starts at `[1]`.

## docs: adopt AGENTS.md (0.63.0)

Commits: [[1]]

The bot-instructions file is renamed `CLAUDE.md` ŌåÆ `AGENTS.md`
so Zed and the broader agent-tooling ecosystem (which now
default to `AGENTS.md`) read it natively. Claude Code still
auto-loads only `CLAUDE.md`, so a one-line `CLAUDE.md` import
shim (`@AGENTS.md`) keeps it working ŌĆö both tools read one
canonical file.

- **Single canonical file** ŌĆö `AGENTS.md` holds the content;
  `CLAUDE.md` is a one-line `@AGENTS.md` import for Claude
  Code's loader. Nothing human-facing points at the shim.
- **Live references repointed** ŌĆö every `CLAUDE.md` mention
  and `#anchor` link in live docs (`README.md`,
  `ARCHITECTURE.md`, `notes/`, the `src/push.rs` pre-commit
  doc comment, and `AGENTS.md` itself) now names `AGENTS.md`,
  so links resolve in editors and on GitHub.
- **History left as written** ŌĆö `CLAUDE.md` prose in
  `chores-01..12` and `done.md` records what was true at the
  time and is untouched; only the live navigational anchor
  links in the `chores-10/11/12` headers
  (`#reference-numbering`) were repointed so they don't
  dangle against the one-line `CLAUDE.md` shim.

### Why an import shim, not a symlink

A symlink `CLAUDE.md ŌåÆ AGENTS.md` also satisfies Claude
Code's loader, but the `@AGENTS.md` import was chosen
because it is explicit and visible in a diff and does not
depend on symlink semantics (git symlink handling,
cross-platform checkout). Separately, GitHub renders a
symlinked `.md` as a stub page rather than the target's
markdown, so a `CLAUDE.md#anchor` link through a symlink
would not resolve ŌĆö moot here since live links now point
straight at `AGENTS.md`, but it reinforces keeping
`CLAUDE.md` a real file.

## docs: tighten after-finalize rule (0.63.1)

Commits: [[2]]

The stop-and-wait rule in `cycle-protocol.md` was titled
`### After finalize: stop and wait` and phrased as "final
words go in the approval prompt *before* executing finalize"
ŌĆö wording that assumes finalize is a separate,
manually-invoked step and names only finalize as the
trigger. With `vc-x1 push` the push and `vc-x1 finalize` on
`.claude` are bundled as the tail stages, so there is no
"before finalize" gap left to speak in once the push is
invoked. After `0.63.0`'s push the bot emitted a closing
summary *after* `vc-x1 push` returned, violating the rule
(and likely riding into the `.claude` commit via the
`--delay 10` window).

- **Renamed to "After push or finalize"** ŌĆö both triggers
  are named, in the title and the paragraph: the remote
  crossing is the push itself, and `vc-x1 finalize` on
  `.claude` is the detached step. This also covers a manual
  `jj git push` (push, no finalize) and a manual
  `vc-x1 finalize` (no wrapper).
- **Wrapper case spelled out** ŌĆö `vc-x1 push` does both as
  tail stages (`push-app`, then `vc-x1 finalize` on
  `.claude`): closing words go *before* invoking the wrapper,
  and the bot does not purposely emit anything after it
  returns. The rule names the `vc-x1 finalize` operation, not
  the wrapper's internal `finalize-claude` stage label.

The bot thinks the original lapse was a compliance slip (the
rule was read and quoted, then broken), not a comprehension
gap; the doc change closes the one genuine ambiguity (the
wrapper bundles push + finalize, leaving no gap to speak in)
rather than expecting prose alone to prevent the slip. The
rule lives only in `cycle-protocol.md`; AGENTS.md already
mandates reading it before commit work, so no duplicate
restatement was added there.

So the *documented* flow routes the detail through `## In Progress` and into chores at close-out ŌĆö it doesn't say the deleted Todo detail lands in chores at Preparation. But the substance of your request is sound: the rich detail (recipe, standardize, caveat bullets) shouldn't be condensed away and lost. input.new_string:
## docs: codify merge-non-ff recipe (0.64.0)

Multi-commit cycles default to the merge-non-ff trapezoid,
but the recipe is split between `cycle-protocol.md` and
AGENTS.md with wrinkles re-derived each time (exercised
manually on `0.62.0`). Codify the full recipe and
standardize the flag spelling. Split out of Idea #1 (the
broader cycle-protocol.md codification).

- Recipe: `jj rebase -r <closeout> --onto <prev-closeout>
  --onto <work-tip>` (first `--onto` = trunk ŌåÆ first
  parent), then `jj new <merge>` to lift `@` above the
  merge, then `jj git push --bookmark main`.
- Standardize on `--onto` / `-o` (canonical), not the
  `-d` alias ŌĆö update AGENTS.md's `-d` spellings (jj
  Basics + the post-amend `jj new` note) to `--onto`,
  plus a one-line "`-d` is an alias of `--onto`" so the
  docs don't read as two different flags.
- `@` reverts to the work-tip's content until the
  `jj new` (the "post-amend `jj new`" gotcha already in
  AGENTS.md).
- Post-hoc caveat: if the cycle was already pushed
  keep-separate (as `0.62.0` was), the rebase needs
  `--ignore-immutable` and the push is a force-update of
  `main`; the standard recipe assumes the merge is set up
  before the close-out push.

# References

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Chores-13

Continuation of `chores-12.md` (closed at `0.62.x`). This
file covers the `0.63.0` cycle onward.

Subsection headers that record a release use the
trailing-version format from AGENTS.md: `## Description (X.Y.Z)`.
Reference numbering is file-local ŌĆö see
[`AGENTS.md`](../../AGENTS.md#reference-numbering); chores-13
starts at `[1]`.

## docs: adopt AGENTS.md (0.63.0)

Commits: [[1]]

The bot-instructions file is renamed `CLAUDE.md` ŌåÆ `AGENTS.md`
so Zed and the broader agent-tooling ecosystem (which now
default to `AGENTS.md`) read it natively. Claude Code still
auto-loads only `CLAUDE.md`, so a one-line `CLAUDE.md` import
shim (`@AGENTS.md`) keeps it working ŌĆö both tools read one
canonical file.

- **Single canonical file** ŌĆö `AGENTS.md` holds the content;
  `CLAUDE.md` is a one-line `@AGENTS.md` import for Claude
  Code's loader. Nothing human-facing points at the shim.
- **Live references repointed** ŌĆö every `CLAUDE.md` mention
  and `#anchor` link in live docs (`README.md`,
  `ARCHITECTURE.md`, `notes/`, the `src/push.rs` pre-commit
  doc comment, and `AGENTS.md` itself) now names `AGENTS.md`,
  so links resolve in editors and on GitHub.
- **History left as written** ŌĆö `CLAUDE.md` prose in
  `chores-01..12` and `done.md` records what was true at the
  time and is untouched; only the live navigational anchor
  links in the `chores-10/11/12` headers
  (`#reference-numbering`) were repointed so they don't
  dangle against the one-line `CLAUDE.md` shim.

### Why an import shim, not a symlink

A symlink `CLAUDE.md ŌåÆ AGENTS.md` also satisfies Claude
Code's loader, but the `@AGENTS.md` import was chosen
because it is explicit and visible in a diff and does not
depend on symlink semantics (git symlink handling,
cross-platform checkout). Separately, GitHub renders a
symlinked `.md` as a stub page rather than the target's
markdown, so a `CLAUDE.md#anchor` link through a symlink
would not resolve ŌĆö moot here since live links now point
straight at `AGENTS.md`, but it reinforces keeping
`CLAUDE.md` a real file.

## docs: tighten after-finalize rule (0.63.1)

Commits: [[2]]

The stop-and-wait rule in `cycle-protocol.md` was titled
`### After finalize: stop and wait` and phrased as "final
words go in the approval prompt *before* executing finalize"
ŌĆö wording that assumes finalize is a separate,
manually-invoked step and names only finalize as the
trigger. With `vc-x1 push` the push and `vc-x1 finalize` on
`.claude` are bundled as the tail stages, so there is no
"before finalize" gap left to speak in once the push is
invoked. After `0.63.0`'s push the bot emitted a closing
summary *after* `vc-x1 push` returned, violating the rule
(and likely riding into the `.claude` commit via the
`--delay 10` window).

- **Renamed to "After push or finalize"** ŌĆö both triggers
  are named, in the title and the paragraph: the remote
  crossing is the push itself, and `vc-x1 finalize` on
  `.claude` is the detached step. This also covers a manual
  `jj git push` (push, no finalize) and a manual
  `vc-x1 finalize` (no wrapper).
- **Wrapper case spelled out** ŌĆö `vc-x1 push` does both as
  tail stages (`push-app`, then `vc-x1 finalize` on
  `.claude`): closing words go *before* invoking the wrapper,
  and the bot does not purposely emit anything after it
  returns. The rule names the `vc-x1 finalize` operation, not
  the wrapper's internal `finalize-claude` stage label.

The bot thinks the original lapse was a compliance slip (the
rule was read and quoted, then broken), not a comprehension
gap; the doc change closes the one genuine ambiguity (the
wrapper bundles push + finalize, leaving no gap to speak in)
rather than expecting prose alone to prevent the slip. The
rule lives only in `cycle-protocol.md`; AGENTS.md already
mandates reading it before commit work, so no duplicate
restatement was added there.

message.content:
The Todo didn't get moved to In Progress, here is the original:

1. **merge-non-ff recipe: codify in `notes/cycle-protocol.md`.**
   Multi-commit cycles default to the merge-non-ff trapezoid,
   but the recipe is split between `cycle-protocol.md` and
   AGENTS.md with wrinkles re-derived each time (exercised
   manually on `0.62.0`). Codify the full recipe and
   standardize the flag spelling. Split out of Idea #1 (the
   broader cycle-protocol.md codification).
   - Recipe: `jj rebase -r <closeout> --onto <prev-closeout>
     --onto <work-tip>` (first `--onto` = trunk ŌåÆ first
     parent), then `jj new <merge>` to lift `@` above the
     merge, then `jj git push --bookmark main`.
   - Standardize on `--onto` / `-o` (canonical), not the
     `-d` alias ŌĆö update AGENTS.md's `-d` spellings (jj
     Basics + the post-amend `jj new` note) to `--onto`,
     plus a one-line "`-d` is an alias of `--onto`" so the
     docs don't read as two different flags.
   - `@` reverts to the work-tip's content until the
     `jj new` (the "post-amend `jj new`" gotcha already in
     AGENTS.md).
   - Post-hoc caveat: if the cycle was already pushed
     keep-separate (as `0.62.0` was), the rebase needs
     `--ignore-immutable` and the push is a force-update of
     `main`; the standard recipe assumes the merge is set up
     before the close-out push.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Chores-13

Continuation of `chores-12.md` (closed at `0.62.x`). This
file covers the `0.63.0` cycle onward.

Subsection headers that record a release use the
trailing-version format from AGENTS.md: `## Description (X.Y.Z)`.
Reference numbering is file-local ŌĆö see
[`AGENTS.md`](../../AGENTS.md#reference-numbering); chores-13
starts at `[1]`.

## docs: adopt AGENTS.md (0.63.0)

Commits: [[1]]

The bot-instructions file is renamed `CLAUDE.md` ŌåÆ `AGENTS.md`
so Zed and the broader agent-tooling ecosystem (which now
default to `AGENTS.md`) read it natively. Claude Code still
auto-loads only `CLAUDE.md`, so a one-line `CLAUDE.md` import
shim (`@AGENTS.md`) keeps it working ŌĆö both tools read one
canonical file.

- **Single canonical file** ŌĆö `AGENTS.md` holds the content;
  `CLAUDE.md` is a one-line `@AGENTS.md` import for Claude
  Code's loader. Nothing human-facing points at the shim.
- **Live references repointed** ŌĆö every `CLAUDE.md` mention
  and `#anchor` link in live docs (`README.md`,
  `ARCHITECTURE.md`, `notes/`, the `src/push.rs` pre-commit
  doc comment, and `AGENTS.md` itself) now names `AGENTS.md`,
  so links resolve in editors and on GitHub.
- **History left as written** ŌĆö `CLAUDE.md` prose in
  `chores-01..12` and `done.md` records what was true at the
  time and is untouched; only the live navigational anchor
  links in the `chores-10/11/12` headers
  (`#reference-numbering`) were repointed so they don't
  dangle against the one-line `CLAUDE.md` shim.

### Why an import shim, not a symlink

A symlink `CLAUDE.md ŌåÆ AGENTS.md` also satisfies Claude
Code's loader, but the `@AGENTS.md` import was chosen
because it is explicit and visible in a diff and does not
depend on symlink semantics (git symlink handling,
cross-platform checkout). Separately, GitHub renders a
symlinked `.md` as a stub page rather than the target's
markdown, so a `CLAUDE.md#anchor` link through a symlink
would not resolve ŌĆö moot here since live links now point
straight at `AGENTS.md`, but it reinforces keeping
`CLAUDE.md` a real file.

## docs: tighten after-finalize rule (0.63.1)

Commits: [[2]]

The stop-and-wait rule in `cycle-protocol.md` was titled
`### After finalize: stop and wait` and phrased as "final
words go in the approval prompt *before* executing finalize"
ŌĆö wording that assumes finalize is a separate,
manually-invoked step and names only finalize as the
trigger. With `vc-x1 push` the push and `vc-x1 finalize` on
`.claude` are bundled as the tail stages, so there is no
"before finalize" gap left to speak in once the push is
invoked. After `0.63.0`'s push the bot emitted a closing
summary *after* `vc-x1 push` returned, violating the rule
(and likely riding into the `.claude` commit via the
`--delay 10` window).

- **Renamed to "After push or finalize"** ŌĆö both triggers
  are named, in the title and the paragraph: the remote
  crossing is the push itself, and `vc-x1 finalize` on
  `.claude` is the detached step. This also covers a manual
  `jj git push` (push, no finalize) and a manual
  `vc-x1 finalize` (no wrapper).
- **Wrapper case spelled out** ŌĆö `vc-x1 push` does both as
  tail stages (`push-app`, then `vc-x1 finalize` on
  `.claude`): closing words go *before* invoking the wrapper,
  and the bot does not purposely emit anything after it
  returns. The rule names the `vc-x1 finalize` operation, not
  the wrapper's internal `finalize-claude` stage label.

The bot thinks the original lapse was a compliance slip (the
rule was read and quoted, then broken), not a comprehension
gap; the doc change closes the one genuine ambiguity (the
wrapper bundles push + finalize, leaving no gap to speak in)
rather than expecting prose alone to prevent the slip. The
rule lives only in `cycle-protocol.md`; AGENTS.md already
mandates reading it before commit work, so no duplicate
restatement was added there.

## docs: codify merge-non-ff recipe (0.64.0)

Multi-commit cycles default to the merge-non-ff trapezoid,
but the recipe is split between `cycle-protocol.md` and
AGENTS.md with wrinkles re-derived each time (exercised
manually on `0.62.0`). Codify the full recipe and
standardize the flag spelling. Split out of Idea #1 (the
broader cycle-protocol.md codification).

- Recipe: `jj rebase -r <closeout> --onto <prev-closeout>
  --onto <work-tip>` (first `--onto` = trunk ŌåÆ first
  parent), then `jj new <merge>` to lift `@` above the
  merge, then `jj git push --bookmark main`.
- Standardize on `--onto` / `-o` (canonical), not the
  `-d` alias ŌĆö update AGENTS.md's `-d` spellings (jj
  Basics + the post-amend `jj new` note) to `--onto`,
  plus a one-line "`-d` is an alias of `--onto`" so the
  docs don't read as two different flags.
- `@` reverts to the work-tip's content until the
  `jj new` (the "post-amend `jj new`" gotcha already in
  AGENTS.md).
- Post-hoc caveat: if the cycle was already pushed
  keep-separate (as `0.62.0` was), the rebase needs
  `--ignore-immutable` and the push is a force-update of
  `main`; the standard recipe assumes the merge is set up
  before the close-out push.

file.content:
### Shape at close-out push

Decide the shape and get user approval before pushing ŌĆö
the choice is hard to undo once on the target. Surface
the options, wait for the user.

- **Squash to one commit** ŌĆö single entry on the target.
  Right for straightforward changes where the Work-N is
  focused on one or two files.
- **Merge non-ff** ŌĆö `main` gains a merge commit
  (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via two
  parents. `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` shows the trapezoidal
  shape. Set up: `jj rebase -r <tip> --onto <prev>
  --onto <sub-tip>` where `<prev>` is the previous
  cycle's closed-out commit (current main tip). Two
  `--onto` ŌåÆ `<tip>` becomes a merge of `<prev>` +
  `<sub-tip>`.
- **Keep separate** ŌĆö one commit per cycle entry on
  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.

Set up squash/merge before invoking `vc-x1 push`; use
`jj git push` directly for non-standard shapes.

### vc-x1 push wrapper

`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps per-push mechanics. See

  --onto <sub-tip>` where `<prev>` is the previous
  cycle's closed-out commit (current main tip). Two
  `--onto` ŌåÆ `<tip>` becomes a merge of `<prev>` +

- **Keep separate** ŌĆö one commit per cycle entry on

Set up squash/merge before invoking `vc-x1 push`; use
`jj git push` directly for non-standard shapes.
input.new_string:
- **Merge non-ff** ŌĆö `main` gains a merge commit
  (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via two
  parents. `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` shows the trapezoidal
  shape. See [Merge non-ff recipe](#merge-non-ff-recipe)
  for the full setup sequence.
- **Keep separate** ŌĆö one commit per cycle entry on
  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.

Set up squash/merge before invoking `vc-x1 push`; use
`jj git push` directly for non-standard shapes.

### Merge non-ff recipe

Setting up a [Merge non-ff](#shape-at-close-out-push)
close-out is a fixed sequence. `<closeout>` is the cycle's
close-out commit, `<prev>` the previous cycle's close-out
(the current `main` tip), `<work-tip>` the cycle's last
Work commit:

1. **Rebase the close-out into a merge** ŌĆö `jj rebase -r
   <closeout> --onto <prev> --onto <work-tip>`. The first
   `--onto` becomes the merge's first parent (trunk); the
   two `--onto` flags together make `<closeout>` a merge of
   `<prev>` + `<work-tip>`.
2. **Lift `@` above the merge** ŌĆö `jj new <merge>`. The
   rebase leaves `@` on the rewritten merge with its
   content reading as uncommitted (the
   [post-amend `jj new`](../AGENTS.md#jj-basics) gotcha);
   `jj new` creates an empty `@` above so the working tree
   reads clean.
3. **Push** ŌĆö `jj git push --bookmark main -R .`.

**Post-hoc caveat.** If the cycle was already pushed
[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) (as `0.62.0`
was), its commits are immutable: the rebase needs
`--ignore-immutable` and the push force-updates `main`.
The standard sequence assumes the merge is set up *before*
the close-out push.
