attachment.snippet:
1	# Cycle protocol
2	
3	This protocol uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form).
4	
5	## Cycles
6	
7	A cycle has three phases:
8	
9	- **[Preparation](#preparation)** (`X.Y.Z-0`) ŌĆö the cycle's
10	  first commit. Sets up the cycle:
11	  - Backfill the previous cycle's chores `Commits:` ref.
12	  - Bump `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
13	  - Pick up a `## Todo` item (typically the top-ranked,
14	    #1) into `## In Progress` (bold title + succinct problem
15	    statement + plan ladder).
16	  - Open the [chores section](#chores-sections).
17	- **[Work-N](#work-n)** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, ŌĆ”) ŌĆö the
18	  commits that implement the change. As many as the change
19	  needs; each runs through the
20	  [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
21	- **[Close-out](#close-out)** (bare `X.Y.Z`) ŌĆö the cycle's
22	  last commit. Bookkeeping only:
23	  - Move the picked-up item to a one-line entry in
24	    `## Done`.
25	  - Move the `## In Progress` block into the
26	    [chores section](#chores-sections).
27	  - Optionally update `notes/README.md` if functionality
28	    changed.
29	
30	A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
31	either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the
32	result must always be published at close-out. See
33	[Pushing](#pushing).
34	
35	**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
36	warrant its own ladder, it subdivides ŌĆö `X.Y.Z-3.0`
37	Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
38	Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
39	at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
40	suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
41	for the local-ladder mechanics.
42	
43	## Chores sections
44	
45	A **chores section** is a `##` section in
46	`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording one cycle:
47	
48	- One section per cycle is the default.
49	- Per-commit sections (like 0.55.0's four) are a
50	  deliberate alternative.
51	
52	The phrase **"Open" the chores section** means append a
53	`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
54	with the cycle's anticipated close-out title (e.g.
55	`## refactor: foo bar (X.Y.Z)`). The body is empty;
56	content arrives at close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out)).
57	
58	Fuller chores conventions (content rules, header sync,
59	design subsection pattern, `Commits:` formatting) live in
60	AGENTS.md [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions).
61	
62	## Preparation
63	
64	The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):
65	
66	- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores section
67	  `Commits:` ref** ŌĆö see
68	  [Chores commit references](../AGENTS.md#chores-commit-references).
69	- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
70	- **Update `Cargo.lock`** to match ŌĆö run `cargo build`
71	  (or `cargo check`) so the lockfile's `vc-x1` version
72	  tracks `Cargo.toml` in the same commit.
73	- **Move a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one) into
74	  `## In Progress` and the todo item should have:
75	  - A **bold title line** ŌĆö that will be the chores
76	    section header, minus the `## ` prefix.
77	  - A **succinct problem statement**; add if one is needed
78	  - A **plan ladder**.
79	- **Open the [chores section](#chores-sections)** ŌĆö
80	  append a `##` header with the cycle's anticipated
81	  close-out title.
82	
83	## Work-N
84	
85	The cycle's work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, ŌĆ”)
86	implement the change. As many as needed:
87	
88	- Each commit runs through the
89	  **[per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow)**.
90	- **Interim pushes** are optional (backup, progress
91	  visibility).
92	- Close-out is the only mandatory push (see
93	  [Pushing](#pushing)).
94	- **Subdivide into a sub-cycle** if a Work commit's
95	  scope grows enough (see
96	  [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)).
97	
98	## Close-out
99	
100	The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) does bookkeeping
101	only ŌĆö the commit body describes that bookkeeping, not
102	what happens post-squash:
103	
104	- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
105	  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
106	- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the
107	  [chores section](#chores-sections)** (the title-only
108	  placeholder opened during Preparation):
109	  - Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
110	    `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line ŌĆö the
111	    chores `##` header already carries that string).
112	  - Paste under the chores `##` header.
113	  - Sync the chores header to the **final** commit title
114	    if the cycle's scope shifted; update every anchor
115	    back-reference.
116	  - Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
117	    commits; optional `### Outcome` notes.
118	  - Replace `## In Progress` with
119	    `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
120	- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
121	  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).
122	
123	Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
124	publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time ŌĆö
125	see [Pushing](#pushing).
126	
127	## Numbering
128	
129	The version suffix on each commit encodes its phase ŌĆö
130	the **final identifier `0` marks a Preparation**:
131	
132	- `X.Y.Z-0` ŌĆö Preparation
133	- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, ŌĆ” ŌĆö Work commits
134	- `X.Y.Z` ŌĆö Close-out (bare version, no suffix)
135	
136	Disambiguation:
137	
138	- `-10` ŌĆö Work commit #10 (final identifier `10`), not a
139	  Preparation.
140	- `-1.0` ŌĆö Preparation of the `-1` sub-cycle (final
141	  identifier `0`).
142	
143	**Nesting.** Sub-cycles append another level, recursively:
144	
145	- `X.Y.Z-3.0` ŌĆö Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle
146	- `X.Y.Z-3.1`, `X.Y.Z-3.2` ŌĆö its Work
147	- `X.Y.Z-3` ŌĆö its Close-out
148	- `X.Y.Z-3.1.0` ŌĆö Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle
149	
150	Sub-cycles needing no Preparation omit `.0` (`-3.1`,
151	`-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later adds
152	`-3.0` without renumbering siblings.
153	
154	Bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase so `vc-x1 -V`
155	reports the active phase at build time.
156	
157	## Per-commit flow
158	
159	Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
160	through:
161	
162	1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
163	   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`.
164	2. **Do the work** (see [Iterative work](#iterative-work)
165	   for the loop-and-squash technique).
166	3. **Flip this commit `(current)` ŌåÆ `(done)`** in `## In
167	   Progress` ŌĆö before the cargo cycle and the commit.
168	4. **Cargo cycle** (skip-able for purely-docs commits; full
169	   cycle mandatory at close-out):
170	   1. `cargo fmt`
171	   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
172	   3. `cargo test`
173	   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
174	   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
175	5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
176	   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
177	   changes and we iterate until complete.
178	6. **Write the commit description** ŌĆö see
179	   [Commit description](#commit-description).
180	7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
181	   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
182	8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
183	   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
184	   keeps the verb visible):
185	
186	   ```
187	   jj commit -m \
188	   "<type>: <short description> (<version>)" \
189	   -m "<intro paragraph>
190	
191	   - file1: gist
192	   - file2: gist
193	
194	   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
195	   -R .
196	   ```
197	
198	**Two overrides apply:**
199	
200	- **Deviation or question** ŌĆö any time the work deviates
201	  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
202	  surface it; don't push through.
203	- **ESC-ESC** ŌĆö the user can interrupt at any point to pull
204	  a review or question forward.
205	
206	## Commit description
207	
208	[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/)
209	with a version suffix:
210	
211	```
212	<type>: <short description> (<version>)
213	```
214	
215	### Title
216	
217	- Ōēż50 chars total (version suffix counts).
218	- Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`,
219	  `docs`, `chore`.
220	- Favor terse phrasings.

content.text:
Reviewed your tweaks to the recipe. A few things to flag before this is commit-ready:

**Step 1** (`### Merge non-ff recipe`):
- "The **two** `--onto <prev>` options become the first parent" ŌĆö there's only *one* `--onto <prev>`. Reads as if both flags are `<prev>`. Intended is: the `--onto <prev>` (first) becomes the first parent (trunk), `--onto <work-tip>` the second.
- "and **froms** a trapezoidal" ŌĆö typo, ŌåÆ "forms".
- "(this changes commits)" ŌĆö unclear what this means; rebasing `<closeout>` rewrites it in place ŌĆö is that the intent? The parenthetical reads ambiguously.

**Step 2:**
- Grammar is broken: "to add an empty `@` node above the **working tree**, See [post-amend `jj new`](ŌĆ”) gotcha**)**;" ŌĆö a mid-sentence capital "See", a dangling `)` with no opening paren, and "above the working tree" should be "above the merge".
- The *why* got trimmed out ŌĆö the cross-ref now points at the gotcha without saying what triggers it (the rebase leaves `@` on the rewritten merge, whose content reads as uncommitted). Without that, a reader doesn't know why `jj new` is needed here.

**Step 3** and the **Post-hoc caveat** read fine (caveat lost the `(as 0.62.0 was)` example ŌĆö your call, fine either way).

Want me to apply fixes for the step 1 "two/froms/changes commits" and the step 2 grammar + restore the one-clause "why," or would you rather hand-edit?

file.content:
### Merge non-ff recipe

Setting up a [Merge non-ff](#shape-at-close-out-push)
close-out is a fixed sequence. `<closeout>` is the cycle's
close-out commit, `<prev>` the previous cycle's close-out
(the current `main` tip), `<work-tip>` the cycle's last
Work commit:

1. **Rebase the close-out into a merge** ŌĆö `jj rebase -r
   <closeout> --onto <prev> --onto <work-tip>`. The
   `--onto <prev>` option becomes the first parent (trunk)
   and `--onto <work-tip>` becomes the second parent (this
   change's commits), together these make `<closeout>` a merge
   of `<prev>` + `<work-tip>` and froms a trapezoidal commit.
2. **Use `jj new <merge>`** to add an empty `@` node above the
   working tree, See
   [post-amend `jj new`](../AGENTS.md#jj-basics) for why.
3. **Push** ŌĆö `jj git push --bookmark main -R .`.

**Post-hoc caveat.** If the cycle was already pushed
[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) its commits are
immutable: the rebase needs `--ignore-immutable` and the
push force-updates `main`.
The standard sequence assumes the merge is set up *before*
the close-out push.

attachment.snippet:
1	# Cycle protocol
2	
3	This protocol uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form).
4	
5	## Cycles
6	
7	A cycle has three phases:
8	
9	- **[Preparation](#preparation)** (`X.Y.Z-0`) ŌĆö the cycle's
10	  first commit. Sets up the cycle:
11	  - Backfill the previous cycle's chores `Commits:` ref.
12	  - Bump `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
13	  - Pick up a `## Todo` item (typically the top-ranked,
14	    #1) into `## In Progress` (bold title + succinct problem
15	    statement + plan ladder).
16	  - Open the [chores section](#chores-sections).
17	- **[Work-N](#work-n)** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, ŌĆ”) ŌĆö the
18	  commits that implement the change. As many as the change
19	  needs; each runs through the
20	  [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
21	- **[Close-out](#close-out)** (bare `X.Y.Z`) ŌĆö the cycle's
22	  last commit. Bookkeeping only:
23	  - Move the picked-up item to a one-line entry in
24	    `## Done`.
25	  - Move the `## In Progress` block into the
26	    [chores section](#chores-sections).
27	  - Optionally update `notes/README.md` if functionality
28	    changed.
29	
30	A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
31	either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the
32	result must always be published at close-out. See
33	[Pushing](#pushing).
34	
35	**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
36	warrant its own ladder, it subdivides ŌĆö `X.Y.Z-3.0`
37	Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
38	Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
39	at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
40	suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
41	for the local-ladder mechanics.
42	
43	## Chores sections
44	
45	A **chores section** is a `##` section in
46	`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording one cycle:
47	
48	- One section per cycle is the default.
49	- Per-commit sections (like 0.55.0's four) are a
50	  deliberate alternative.
51	
52	The phrase **"Open" the chores section** means append a
53	`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
54	with the cycle's anticipated close-out title (e.g.
55	`## refactor: foo bar (X.Y.Z)`). The body is empty;
56	content arrives at close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out)).
57	
58	Fuller chores conventions (content rules, header sync,
59	design subsection pattern, `Commits:` formatting) live in
60	AGENTS.md [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions).
61	
62	## Preparation
63	
64	The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):
65	
66	- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores section
67	  `Commits:` ref** ŌĆö see
68	  [Chores commit references](../AGENTS.md#chores-commit-references).
69	- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
70	- **Update `Cargo.lock`** to match ŌĆö run `cargo build`
71	  (or `cargo check`) so the lockfile's `vc-x1` version
72	  tracks `Cargo.toml` in the same commit.
73	- **Move a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one) into
74	  `## In Progress` and the todo item should have:
75	  - A **bold title line** ŌĆö that will be the chores
76	    section header, minus the `## ` prefix.
77	  - A **succinct problem statement**; add if one is needed
78	  - A **plan ladder**.
79	- **Open the [chores section](#chores-sections)** ŌĆö
80	  append a `##` header with the cycle's anticipated
81	  close-out title.
82	
83	## Work-N
84	
85	The cycle's work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, ŌĆ”)
86	implement the change. As many as needed:
87	
88	- Each commit runs through the
89	  **[per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow)**.
90	- **Interim pushes** are optional (backup, progress
91	  visibility).
92	- Close-out is the only mandatory push (see
93	  [Pushing](#pushing)).
94	- **Subdivide into a sub-cycle** if a Work commit's
95	  scope grows enough (see
96	  [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)).
97	
98	## Close-out
99	
100	The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) does bookkeeping
101	only ŌĆö the commit body describes that bookkeeping, not
102	what happens post-squash:
103	
104	- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
105	  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
106	- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the
107	  [chores section](#chores-sections)** (the title-only
108	  placeholder opened during Preparation):
109	  - Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
110	    `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line ŌĆö the
111	    chores `##` header already carries that string).
112	  - Paste under the chores `##` header.
113	  - Sync the chores header to the **final** commit title
114	    if the cycle's scope shifted; update every anchor
115	    back-reference.
116	  - Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
117	    commits; optional `### Outcome` notes.
118	  - Replace `## In Progress` with
119	    `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
120	- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
121	  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).
122	
123	Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
124	publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time ŌĆö
125	see [Pushing](#pushing).
126	
127	## Numbering
128	
129	The version suffix on each commit encodes its phase ŌĆö
130	the **final identifier `0` marks a Preparation**:
131	
132	- `X.Y.Z-0` ŌĆö Preparation
133	- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, ŌĆ” ŌĆö Work commits
134	- `X.Y.Z` ŌĆö Close-out (bare version, no suffix)
135	
136	Disambiguation:
137	
138	- `-10` ŌĆö Work commit #10 (final identifier `10`), not a
139	  Preparation.
140	- `-1.0` ŌĆö Preparation of the `-1` sub-cycle (final
141	  identifier `0`).
142	
143	**Nesting.** Sub-cycles append another level, recursively:
144	
145	- `X.Y.Z-3.0` ŌĆö Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle
146	- `X.Y.Z-3.1`, `X.Y.Z-3.2` ŌĆö its Work
147	- `X.Y.Z-3` ŌĆö its Close-out
148	- `X.Y.Z-3.1.0` ŌĆö Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle
149	
150	Sub-cycles needing no Preparation omit `.0` (`-3.1`,
151	`-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later adds
152	`-3.0` without renumbering siblings.
153	
154	Bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase so `vc-x1 -V`
155	reports the active phase at build time.
156	
157	## Per-commit flow
158	
159	Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
160	through:
161	
162	1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
163	   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`.
164	2. **Do the work** (see [Iterative work](#iterative-work)
165	   for the loop-and-squash technique).
166	3. **Flip this commit `(current)` ŌåÆ `(done)`** in `## In
167	   Progress` ŌĆö before the cargo cycle and the commit.

169	   cycle mandatory at close-out):
170	   1. `cargo fmt`
171	   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
172	   3. `cargo test`
173	   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
174	   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
175	5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
176	   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
177	   changes and we iterate until complete.
178	6. **Write the commit description** ŌĆö see
179	   [Commit description](#commit-description).
180	7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
181	   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
182	8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
183	   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
184	   keeps the verb visible):
185	
186	   ```
187	   jj commit -m \
188	   "<type>: <short description> (<version>)" \
189	   -m "<intro paragraph>
190	
191	   - file1: gist
192	   - file2: gist
193	
194	   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
195	   -R .
196	   ```
197	
198	**Two overrides apply:**
199

201	  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
202	  surface it; don't push through.
203	- **ESC-ESC** ŌĆö the user can interrupt at any point to pull
204	  a review or question forward.

210

218	- Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`,

303	close-out is a fixed sequence. `<closeout>` is the cycle's

310	   - `-r <closeout>` keeps the `<closeout>` commit in place.
311	   - `--onto <prev>` becomes its first parent (trunk).
312	   - `--onto <work-tip>` the second parent.
313	   together these make `<closeout>` a merge of

316	   the merge. See
317	   [post-amend `jj new`](../AGENTS.md#jj-basics) for why.

file.content:
### Merge non-ff recipe

Setting up a [Merge non-ff](#shape-at-close-out-push)
close-out is a fixed sequence. `<closeout>` is the cycle's
close-out commit, `<prev>` the previous cycle's close-out
(the current `main` tip), `<work-tip>` the cycle's last
Work commit:

1. **Rebase the close-out into a merge** ŌĆö `jj rebase -r
   <closeout> --onto <prev> --onto <work-tip>`.
   - `-r <closeout>` keeps the `<closeout>` commit in place.
   - `--onto <prev>` becomes its first parent (trunk).
   - `--onto <work-tip>` the second parent.
   together these make `<closeout>` a merge of
   `<prev>` + `<work-tip>` and form a trapezoidal commit.
2. **Use `jj new <merge>`** to add an empty `@` node above
   the merge. See
   [post-amend `jj new`](../AGENTS.md#jj-basics) for why.
3. **Push** ŌĆö `jj git push --bookmark main -R .`.

**Post-hoc caveat.** If the cycle was already pushed
[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) its commits are
immutable: the rebase needs `--ignore-immutable` and the
push force-updates `main`.
The standard sequence assumes the merge is set up *before*
the close-out push.

### vc-x1 push wrapper

`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps per-push mechanics. See

1	# Cycle protocol
2	
3	This protocol uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form).
4	
5	## Cycles
6	
7	A cycle has three phases:
8	
9	- **[Preparation](#preparation)** (`X.Y.Z-0`) ŌĆö the cycle's
10	  first commit. Sets up the cycle:
11	  - Backfill the previous cycle's chores `Commits:` ref.
12	  - Bump `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
13	  - Pick up a `## Todo` item (typically the top-ranked,
14	    #1) into `## In Progress` (bold title + succinct problem
15	    statement + plan ladder).
16	  - Open the [chores section](#chores-sections).
17	- **[Work-N](#work-n)** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, ŌĆ”) ŌĆö the
18	  commits that implement the change. As many as the change
19	  needs; each runs through the
20	  [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
21	- **[Close-out](#close-out)** (bare `X.Y.Z`) ŌĆö the cycle's
22	  last commit. Bookkeeping only:
23	  - Move the picked-up item to a one-line entry in
24	    `## Done`.
25	  - Move the `## In Progress` block into the
26	    [chores section](#chores-sections).
27	  - Optionally update `notes/README.md` if functionality
28	    changed.
29	
30	A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
31	either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the
32	result must always be published at close-out. See
33	[Pushing](#pushing).
34	
35	**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
36	warrant its own ladder, it subdivides ŌĆö `X.Y.Z-3.0`
37	Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
38	Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
39	at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
40	suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
41	for the local-ladder mechanics.
42	
43	## Chores sections
44	
45	A **chores section** is a `##` section in
46	`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording one cycle:
47	
48	- One section per cycle is the default.
49	- Per-commit sections (like 0.55.0's four) are a
50	  deliberate alternative.
51	
52	The phrase **"Open" the chores section** means append a
53	`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
54	with the cycle's anticipated close-out title (e.g.
55	`## refactor: foo bar (X.Y.Z)`). The body is empty;
56	content arrives at close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out)).
57	
58	Fuller chores conventions (content rules, header sync,
59	design subsection pattern, `Commits:` formatting) live in
60	AGENTS.md [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions).
61	
62	## Preparation
63	
64	The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):
65	
66	- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores section
67	  `Commits:` ref** ŌĆö see
68	  [Chores commit references](../AGENTS.md#chores-commit-references).
69	- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
70	- **Update `Cargo.lock`** to match ŌĆö run `cargo build`
71	  (or `cargo check`) so the lockfile's `vc-x1` version
72	  tracks `Cargo.toml` in the same commit.
73	- **Move a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one) into
74	  `## In Progress` and the todo item should have:
75	  - A **bold title line** ŌĆö that will be the chores
76	    section header, minus the `## ` prefix.
77	  - A **succinct problem statement**; add if one is needed
78	  - A **plan ladder**.
79	- **Open the [chores section](#chores-sections)** ŌĆö
80	  append a `##` header with the cycle's anticipated
81	  close-out title.
82	
83	## Work-N
84	
85	The cycle's work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, ŌĆ”)
86	implement the change. As many as needed:
87	
88	- Each commit runs through the
89	  **[per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow)**.
90	- **Interim pushes** are optional (backup, progress
91	  visibility).
92	- Close-out is the only mandatory push (see
93	  [Pushing](#pushing)).
94	- **Subdivide into a sub-cycle** if a Work commit's
95	  scope grows enough (see
96	  [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)).
97	
98	## Close-out
99	
100	The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) does bookkeeping
101	only ŌĆö the commit body describes that bookkeeping, not
102	what happens post-squash:
103	
104	- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
105	  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
106	- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the
107	  [chores section](#chores-sections)** (the title-only
108	  placeholder opened during Preparation):
109	  - Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
110	    `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line ŌĆö the
111	    chores `##` header already carries that string).
112	  - Paste under the chores `##` header.
113	  - Sync the chores header to the **final** commit title
114	    if the cycle's scope shifted; update every anchor
115	    back-reference.
116	  - Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
117	    commits; optional `### Outcome` notes.
118	  - Replace `## In Progress` with
119	    `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
120	- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
121	  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).
122	
123	Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
124	publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time ŌĆö
125	see [Pushing](#pushing).
126	
127	## Numbering
128	
129	The version suffix on each commit encodes its phase ŌĆö
130	the **final identifier `0` marks a Preparation**:
131	
132	- `X.Y.Z-0` ŌĆö Preparation
133	- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, ŌĆ” ŌĆö Work commits
134	- `X.Y.Z` ŌĆö Close-out (bare version, no suffix)
135	
136	Disambiguation:
137	
138	- `-10` ŌĆö Work commit #10 (final identifier `10`), not a
139	  Preparation.
140	- `-1.0` ŌĆö Preparation of the `-1` sub-cycle (final
141	  identifier `0`).
142	
143	**Nesting.** Sub-cycles append another level, recursively:
144	
145	- `X.Y.Z-3.0` ŌĆö Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle
146	- `X.Y.Z-3.1`, `X.Y.Z-3.2` ŌĆö its Work
147	- `X.Y.Z-3` ŌĆö its Close-out
148	- `X.Y.Z-3.1.0` ŌĆö Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle
149	
150	Sub-cycles needing no Preparation omit `.0` (`-3.1`,
151	`-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later adds
152	`-3.0` without renumbering siblings.
153	
154	Bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase so `vc-x1 -V`
155	reports the active phase at build time.
156	
157	## Per-commit flow
158	
159	Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
160	through:
161	
162	1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
163	   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`.
164	2. **Do the work** (see [Iterative work](#iterative-work)
165	   for the loop-and-squash technique).
166	3. **Flip this commit `(current)` ŌåÆ `(done)`** in `## In
167	   Progress` ŌĆö before the cargo cycle and the commit.
168	4. **Cargo cycle** (skip-able for purely-docs commits; full
169	   cycle mandatory at close-out):
170	   1. `cargo fmt`
171	   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
172	   3. `cargo test`
173	   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
174	   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
175	5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
176	   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
177	   changes and we iterate until complete.
178	6. **Write the commit description** ŌĆö see
179	   [Commit description](#commit-description).
180	7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
181	   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
182	8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
183	   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
184	   keeps the verb visible):
185	
186	   ```
187	   jj commit -m \
188	   "<type>: <short description> (<version>)" \
189	   -m "<intro paragraph>
190	
191	   - file1: gist
192	   - file2: gist
193	
194	   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
195	   -R .
196	   ```
197	
198	**Two overrides apply:**
199	
200	- **Deviation or question** ŌĆö any time the work deviates
201	  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
202	  surface it; don't push through.
203	- **ESC-ESC** ŌĆö the user can interrupt at any point to pull
204	  a review or question forward.
205	
206	## Commit description
207	
208	[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/)
209	with a version suffix:
210	
211	```
212	<type>: <short description> (<version>)
213	```
214	
215	### Title
216	
217	- Ōēż50 chars total (version suffix counts).
218	- Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`,
219	  `docs`, `chore`.
220	- Favor terse phrasings.

content.text:
Honest review: I'd flag this one. The subject is "these" (the three flags), and the sentence has two parallel verbs sharing it ŌĆö "these **make** `<closeout>` a merge ŌĆ” and **form** a trapezoidal commit." With the plural "these," both verbs take the no-`s` form, so "forms" disagrees with "make."

"forms" only works if you reframe the second clause to a singular subject, e.g. "ŌĆ” make `<closeout>` a merge of `<prev>` + `<work-tip>`, which **forms** a trapezoidal commit." As written (`and forms`), it's a subject-verb agreement mismatch.

So: keep `form` (parallel with `make`), or restructure to `, which forms`. Your call ŌĆö which do you want?

attachment.snippet:
1	# AGENTS.md - Bot Instructions
2	
3	## Project Structure
4	
5	This project uses **two separate jj-git repos**:
6	
7	1. **App repo** (`/` ŌĆö project root): Contains the application source code.
8	2. **Bot session repo** (`/.claude/`): Contains Claude Code session data.
9	
10	Both repos are managed with `jj` (Jujutsu), which coexists with git.
11	
12	## Repo Paths (relative from project root)
13	
14	- App repo: `.` (project root)
15	- Bot session repo: `.claude`
16	  (symlink from `~/.claude/projects/<path-to-project-root>/.claude`)
17	
18	## Working Directory
19	
20	Prefer staying in the project root. Use `-R` flags or absolute paths
21	to target other directories rather than `cd`. If `cd` seems necessary,
22	discuss with the user first ŌĆö losing track of cwd causes subtle
23	command failures downstream.
24	
25	When invoking shell commands, prefer the shortest unambiguous path ŌĆö
26	usually relative to cwd (`ls notes/`, not
27	`ls /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1/notes/`). Long absolute paths
28	clutter the transcript without adding information. Out-of-workspace
29	paths (`/tmp/...`, `~/.config/...`) stay absolute. Read/Edit/Write
30	tool args also stay absolute ŌĆö that's a tool-boundary constraint,
31	not a stylistic choice.
32	
33	## File reads ŌĆö read the slice you need
34	
35	Long notes files are appended to over time. Read only the
36	slice your task needs; grep or read further on demand.
37	
38	- **`notes/todo.md`** (the routine acquaint read) ŌĆö first
39	  ~60 lines covers intro + `## In Progress` + the top of the
40	  ranked `## Todo` (priorities, #1 highest). `Read` with
41	  `offset=0, limit=60`. `## Ideas` sits below `## Todo`; read
42	  further only when chasing a lower-ranked entry, an Idea, a
43	  `[N]` ref, or auditing the whole list.
44	- **`notes/todo-backlog.md`** ŌĆö the long-tail backlog
45	  (lower-priority entries below the ranked `## Todo`). Read
46	  only when picking up a backlog item; grep to locate it
47	  first.
48	- **`notes/bugs.md`** ŌĆö the bug list. Small; read whole
49	  when triaging a bug or chasing the `## Bugs` pointer in
50	  todo.md.
51	- **`notes/done.md`** + **`notes/chores/chores-NN.md`** ŌĆö
52	  historical / append-mostly. Scan headings first
53	  (`grep '^## ' notes/chores/chores-NN.md`), then read only
54	  the section you need.
55	
56	**Why:** before the 0.58.0 split, `notes/todo.md` ran ~370
57	lines and grew every cycle. The split moved the backlog and
58	bugs into sibling files so the routine read stays small; the
59	same "slice you need" rule applies to historical files.
60	
61	## Memory
62	
63	Do not use the bot's per-project memory directory
64	(`~/.claude/projects/<path>/memory/`). In a dual-repo setup with
65	AGENTS.md it provides no capability AGENTS.md doesn't already cover,
66	and it loses on discoverability:
67	
68	- **AGENTS.md** ŌĆö at the repo root, a well-known location for bot
69	  instructions, committed, reviewable, visible to every collaborator
70	  (human or bot).
71	- **Memory directory** ŌĆö hidden under the user's home, tied to one
72	  machine, invisible to anyone but the bot, never diffed or reviewed.
73	
74	Easy for everyone to find beats convenient for the bot alone. Put
75	durable context in AGENTS.md (or committed `notes/`) instead.
76	
77	## Speculation marker
78	
79	Durable text the bot writes ŌĆö AGENTS.md, `notes/`, commit bodies,
80	chores sections ŌĆö should stick to observations and direct descriptions
81	of the code or data. If a mechanism, hypothesis, or causal claim
82	enters the text, prefix it with "The bot thinks ..." so a reader can
83	tell the measured from the inferred.
84	
85	**Why:** unmarked speculation reads like evidence, and a future reader
86	(or the bot on a later session) can pick it up as a known fact when
87	it's not. Measured / inferred is a distinction worth keeping visible
88	in the written record.
89	
90	**How to apply:** observations and factual descriptions need no
91	marker. Prefix with "The bot thinks ..." (or a close variant like
92	"The bot's guess is ...") when the claim is a mechanism ("X wins
93	because Y caches better"), a cause ("the drift was due to thermal
94	state"), a prediction ("this should scale linearly"), or any
95	reasoning not directly supported by the data on hand.
96	
97	## jj Basics
98	
99	- `jj st -R .` / `jj st -R .claude` ŌĆö show working copy status
100	- `jj log -R .` / `jj log -R .claude` ŌĆö show commit log
101	- `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` ŌĆö finalize working copy into a commit
102	- `jj describe -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` ŌĆö set description without committing
103	- `jj git push --bookmark <name> -R <repo>` ŌĆö push a bookmark (no
104	  `--allow-new` flag; jj pushes new bookmarks without special flags)
105	- In jj, the working copy (@) is always a mutable commit being edited.
106	  `jj commit` finalizes it and creates a new empty working copy on top.
107	- The `.claude` repo always has uncommitted changes during an active
108	  session because session data updates continuously.
109	- `jj rebase` uses `--onto`/`-o` to name the destination(s).
110	- **Post-amend `jj new`** ŌĆö `jj edit <rev>`,
111	  `jj rebase -r <rev> --onto ...`, and `jj squash --into <rev>`
112	  all leave `@` on the rewritten commit rather than on an
113	  empty change above. The most common case is the close-out
114	  push for a [Merge (non-FF)](notes/cycle-protocol.md#pushing) shape (`jj rebase
115	  -r <tip> --onto <prev> --onto <sub-tip>` leaves `@` on the
116	  new merge). In a colocated jj+git repo, git then detaches
117	  HEAD at the commit's first parent, so `git status`,
118	  gitk, and IDE diff views read the commit's content as
119	  uncommitted relative to HEAD. Follow up with `jj new` to
120	  create an empty `@` above; HEAD shifts to the commit and
121	  the working tree reads clean.
122	
123	## Prose form
124	
125	Long-lived prose on this project follows one basic shape: a short intro
126	that explains the *why* or the high-level *what*, then a `-` bullet
127	list for the details. Wrap lines at Ōēż72 cols (bullet continuations
128	indent two spaces). Avoid wall-of-prose paragraphs ŌĆö they hide the
129	structure that bullets make scannable.
130	
131	Surfaces that use this shape:
132	
133	- Module / function / struct / field doc comments in `.rs` files ŌĆö
134	  see [Doc comments](#doc-comments-on-every-file-function-and-method).
135	- Commit message bodies (both app-repo and session-repo). The
136	  Ōēż50-col title is the commit-specific add-on; see
137	  [Per-commit flow](notes/cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow).
138	- Chore descriptions in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` ŌĆö see
139	  [Chores section content](#chores-section-content--no-edit-list-git-is-the-record).
140	- Todo and Done entries in `notes/todo.md` when an entry needs more
141	  than one line of detail. Pure one-liners are still fine.
142	
143	Bullet *content* differs by surface:
144	
145	- **Commit bodies** ŌĆö bullets are file-by-file: one bullet per file
146	  changed, file plus a one-line gist (e.g.
147	  `README.md: new Overview intro`). Source of truth for the
148	  mechanical edit list.
149	- **Chores / todo / done** ŌĆö bullets are conceptual (design points,
150	  structural notes, the "what landed and why" at a notch above
151	  file-list granularity). Never a copy of the commit's edit list ŌĆö
152	  see [Chores section content](#chores-section-content--no-edit-list-git-is-the-record).
153	- **Doc comments** ŌĆö bullets are whatever structure fits (fields,
154	  cases, invariants).
155	
156	**Problem + plan shape.** `## In Progress` cycle blocks,
157	chores section intros, and `## Todo` entries use a sharper
158	form of the same shape:
159	
160	- **Problem statement** (the why) ŌĆö one or two sentences;
161	  don't pad with intent, don't restate the plan.
162	- **Plan bullets** (the what/when) ŌĆö formality differs by
163	  surface:
164	  - In Progress / chores: numbered ladder (`-N` suffixes,
165	    `(current)` / `(done)` markers) ŌĆö a committed sequence.
166	  - Todo entries: rough informal bullets, no numbering;
167	    formalized only when the entry is picked up into a
168	    cycle.
169	
170	**Semicolons inside bullets.** A bullet that joins

content.text:
The alias line is trimmed ŌĆö good.

On sub-bulleting the three commands: my honest take is it's a toss-up that leans slightly toward **keeping it inline**. The Prose-form rule targets *semicolon-joined clauses* ("a list hiding inside running prose"), and explicitly exempts "very short clauses." Here it's three short command tokens that are the grammatical *subject* of one sentence ("X, Y, and Z all leave `@` onŌĆ”"). Breaking them out forces a cataphoric lead-in ("these all leave `@`ŌĆ”:") before the reader knows what "these" are, which slightly hurts flow.

That said, the sub-bullet version *is* more scannable and you clearly value that. Here's how it'd read so you can compare:

```
- **Post-amend `jj new`** ŌĆö these commands all leave `@` on
  the rewritten commit rather than on an empty change above:
  - `jj edit <rev>`
  - `jj rebase -r <rev> --onto ...`
  - `jj squash --into <rev>`

  The most common case is the close-out push for a
  [Merge (non-FF)](notes/cycle-protocol.md#pushing) shape
  (`jj rebase -r <tip> --onto <prev> --onto <sub-tip>`
  leaves `@` on the new merge). In a colocated jj+git repo,
  git then detaches HEAD ŌĆ”
```

Want me to apply that, or leave the commands inline?

problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" ŌĆö a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)

**docs: codify merge-non-ff recipe (0.64.0)**

Multi-commit cycles default to the merge-non-ff trapezoid,
but the recipe is split between `cycle-protocol.md` and
AGENTS.md with wrinkles re-derived each time (exercised
manually on `0.62.0`). Codify the full recipe and
standardize the flag spelling. Split out of Idea #1 (the
broader cycle-protocol.md codification).

- Recipe: `jj rebase -r <closeout> --onto <prev-closeout>
  --onto <work-tip>` (first `--onto` = trunk ŌåÆ first
  parent), then `jj new <merge>` to lift `@` above the
  merge, then `jj git push --bookmark main`.
- Standardize on `--onto` / `-o` (canonical), not the
  `-d` alias ŌĆö update AGENTS.md's `-d` spellings (jj
  Basics + the post-amend `jj new` note) to `--onto`,
  plus a one-line "`-d` is an alias of `--onto`" so the
  docs don't read as two different flags.
- `@` reverts to the work-tip's content until the
  `jj new` (the "post-amend `jj new`" gotcha already in
  AGENTS.md).
- Post-hoc caveat: if the cycle was already pushed
  keep-separate (as `0.62.0` was), the rebase needs
  `--ignore-immutable` and the push is a force-update of
  `main`; the standard recipe assumes the merge is set up
  before the close-out push.

- 0.64.0-2 AGENTS.md: standardize `-d` ŌåÆ `--onto` in the
  post-amend note; add a one-line "`-d` is an alias of
  `--onto`". (done)
- 0.64.0 close-out and validation.

## docs: codify merge-non-ff recipe (0.64.0)

Multi-commit cycles default to the merge-non-ff trapezoid,
but the recipe is split between `cycle-protocol.md` and
AGENTS.md with wrinkles re-derived each time (exercised
manually on `0.62.0`). Codify the full recipe and
standardize the flag spelling. Split out of Idea #1 (the
broader cycle-protocol.md codification).

- Recipe: `jj rebase -r <closeout> --onto <prev-closeout>
  --onto <work-tip>` (first `--onto` = trunk ŌåÆ first
  parent), then `jj new <merge>` to lift `@` above the
  merge, then `jj git push --bookmark main`.
- Standardize on `--onto` / `-o` (canonical), not the
  `-d` alias ŌĆö update AGENTS.md's `-d` spellings (jj
  Basics + the post-amend `jj new` note) to `--onto`.
- `@` reverts to the work-tip's content until the
  `jj new` (the "post-amend `jj new`" gotcha already in
  AGENTS.md).
- Post-hoc caveat: if the cycle was already pushed
  keep-separate (as `0.62.0` was), the rebase needs
  `--ignore-immutable` and the push is a force-update of
  `main`; the standard recipe assumes the merge is set up
  before the close-out push.

### As-built ladder

- 0.64.0-0 Preparation ŌĆö version bump, 0.63.1 `Commits:`
  backfill, item pickup. Also clarified the Preparation
  step itself: a Cargo.lock-update bullet, reworded the
  Todo "move into In Progress", and a Prose-form intro
  line at the top of `cycle-protocol.md`.
- 0.64.0-1 cycle-protocol.md ŌĆö promote the recipe to a
  `### Merge non-ff recipe` subsection (rebase ŌåÆ `jj new`
  lift ŌåÆ push, with the parents broken out as sub-bullets
  and a post-hoc caveat); trim the shape bullet to point
  at it.
- 0.64.0-2 AGENTS.md ŌĆö standardize `-d` ŌåÆ `--onto` in the
  post-amend note (both rebase examples); add a jj Basics
  bullet for the `--onto`/`-o` spelling; sub-bullet the
  post-amend command list.

# References

content.text:
Good idea ŌĆö I agree, with one caveat. Here's my read:

The post-amend note serves two roles: (1) the **general mechanism** (rebase/edit/squash leave `@` on the rewritten commit ŌåÆ git detaches HEAD ŌåÆ fix with `jj new`), and (2) an **illustrative example** (the merge close-out, with its own `jj rebase ŌĆ” --onto ŌĆ”` snippet). The cycle-protocol recipe now links *back* here for the "why" (step 2: "See post-amend `jj new` for why").

So:
- **Keep** the mechanism sentences ("In a colocated jj+git repo, git then detaches HEAD ŌĆ” reads clean") ŌĆö that's the canonical "why" the recipe points at.
- **Replace** the example sentence ŌĆö `The most common case is the close-out push for a [Merge (non-FF)](ŌĆ”#pushing) shape (`jj rebase -r <tip> --onto <prev> --onto <sub-tip>` leaves `@` on the new merge).` ŌĆö with a tighter pointer to the precise anchor, dropping the duplicated rebase snippet (it now lives in the recipe):

  > The most common case is a [Merge non-ff recipe](notes/cycle-protocol.md#merge-non-ff-recipe) close-out.

That removes the duplicate `jj rebase` example, upgrades `#pushing` ŌåÆ `#merge-non-ff-recipe`, and isn't circular (recipeŌåÆAGENTS for the *mechanism*, AGENTSŌåÆrecipe for the *full sequence*).

One mechanics note: this edits AGENTS.md *content*, which belongs with the `0.64.0-2` commit, not the bookkeeping-only close-out. Since `0.64.0-2` is committed but unpushed, I'd **fold this into `0.64.0-2`** (`jj squash` after editing) rather than slipping content into the close-out.

Want me to apply it that way?

## docs: codify merge-non-ff recipe (0.64.0)

Multi-commit cycles default to the merge-non-ff trapezoid,
but the recipe is split between `cycle-protocol.md` and
AGENTS.md with wrinkles re-derived each time (exercised
manually on `0.62.0`). Codify the full recipe and
standardize the flag spelling. Split out of Idea #1 (the
broader cycle-protocol.md codification).

- Recipe: `jj rebase -r <closeout> --onto <prev-closeout>
  --onto <work-tip>` (first `--onto` = trunk ŌåÆ first
  parent), then `jj new <merge>` to lift `@` above the
  merge, then `jj git push --bookmark main`.
- Standardize on `--onto` / `-o` (canonical), not the
  `-d` alias ŌĆö update AGENTS.md's `-d` spellings (jj
  Basics + the post-amend `jj new` note) to `--onto`.
- `@` reverts to the work-tip's content until the
  `jj new` (the "post-amend `jj new`" gotcha already in
  AGENTS.md).
- Post-hoc caveat: if the cycle was already pushed
  keep-separate (as `0.62.0` was), the rebase needs
  `--ignore-immutable` and the push is a force-update of
  `main`; the standard recipe assumes the merge is set up
  before the close-out push.

# Chores-13

Continuation of `chores-12.md` (closed at `0.62.x`). This
file covers the `0.63.0` cycle onward.

Subsection headers that record a release use the
trailing-version format from AGENTS.md: `## Description (X.Y.Z)`.
Reference numbering is file-local ŌĆö see
[`AGENTS.md`](../../AGENTS.md#reference-numbering); chores-13
starts at `[1]`.

## docs: adopt AGENTS.md (0.63.0)

Commits: [[1]]

The bot-instructions file is renamed `CLAUDE.md` ŌåÆ `AGENTS.md`
so Zed and the broader agent-tooling ecosystem (which now
default to `AGENTS.md`) read it natively. Claude Code still
auto-loads only `CLAUDE.md`, so a one-line `CLAUDE.md` import
shim (`@AGENTS.md`) keeps it working ŌĆö both tools read one
canonical file.

- **Single canonical file** ŌĆö `AGENTS.md` holds the content;
  `CLAUDE.md` is a one-line `@AGENTS.md` import for Claude
  Code's loader. Nothing human-facing points at the shim.
- **Live references repointed** ŌĆö every `CLAUDE.md` mention
  and `#anchor` link in live docs (`README.md`,
  `ARCHITECTURE.md`, `notes/`, the `src/push.rs` pre-commit
  doc comment, and `AGENTS.md` itself) now names `AGENTS.md`,
  so links resolve in editors and on GitHub.
- **History left as written** ŌĆö `CLAUDE.md` prose in
  `chores-01..12` and `done.md` records what was true at the
  time and is untouched; only the live navigational anchor
  links in the `chores-10/11/12` headers
  (`#reference-numbering`) were repointed so they don't
  dangle against the one-line `CLAUDE.md` shim.

### Why an import shim, not a symlink

A symlink `CLAUDE.md ŌåÆ AGENTS.md` also satisfies Claude
Code's loader, but the `@AGENTS.md` import was chosen
because it is explicit and visible in a diff and does not
depend on symlink semantics (git symlink handling,
cross-platform checkout). Separately, GitHub renders a
symlinked `.md` as a stub page rather than the target's
markdown, so a `CLAUDE.md#anchor` link through a symlink
would not resolve ŌĆö moot here since live links now point
straight at `AGENTS.md`, but it reinforces keeping
`CLAUDE.md` a real file.

## docs: tighten after-finalize rule (0.63.1)

Commits: [[2]]

The stop-and-wait rule in `cycle-protocol.md` was titled
`### After finalize: stop and wait` and phrased as "final
words go in the approval prompt *before* executing finalize"
ŌĆö wording that assumes finalize is a separate,
manually-invoked step and names only finalize as the
trigger. With `vc-x1 push` the push and `vc-x1 finalize` on
`.claude` are bundled as the tail stages, so there is no
"before finalize" gap left to speak in once the push is
invoked. After `0.63.0`'s push the bot emitted a closing
summary *after* `vc-x1 push` returned, violating the rule
(and likely riding into the `.claude` commit via the
`--delay 10` window).

- **Renamed to "After push or finalize"** ŌĆö both triggers
  are named, in the title and the paragraph: the remote
  crossing is the push itself, and `vc-x1 finalize` on
  `.claude` is the detached step. This also covers a manual
  `jj git push` (push, no finalize) and a manual
  `vc-x1 finalize` (no wrapper).
- **Wrapper case spelled out** ŌĆö `vc-x1 push` does both as
  tail stages (`push-app`, then `vc-x1 finalize` on
  `.claude`): closing words go *before* invoking the wrapper,
  and the bot does not purposely emit anything after it
  returns. The rule names the `vc-x1 finalize` operation, not
  the wrapper's internal `finalize-claude` stage label.

The bot thinks the original lapse was a compliance slip (the
rule was read and quoted, then broken), not a comprehension
gap; the doc change closes the one genuine ambiguity (the
wrapper bundles push + finalize, leaving no gap to speak in)
rather than expecting prose alone to prevent the slip. The
rule lives only in `cycle-protocol.md`; AGENTS.md already
mandates reading it before commit work, so no duplicate
restatement was added there.

content.text:
That reframing is better than what's there ŌĆö you've put your finger on the real point. Two distinct "why"s were tangled together:

- **Why the merge-non-ff shape** ŌĆö the trapezoid defines the cycle's commit range visually (vs. a flat linear series). That's the *shape's* rationale, and it lives in the recipe / the example sentence we just edited.
- **Why `jj new`** ŌĆö it's not merge-specific at all. An empty `@` on top is just **jj's normal final form**. Any in-place rewrite (`edit`/`rebase`/`squash`) leaves `@` *on* the commit, and `jj new` restores the usual empty-`@`-on-top. The git-detaches-HEAD mechanism is incidental jj plumbing, not the point.

So instead of the dense mechanism paragraph, lead with that truth and keep the symptom only as reassurance:

```
- **Post-amend `jj new`** ŌĆö jj's working copy is always an
  empty `@` on top of the tree. Commands that rewrite a
  commit in place leave `@` *on* the rewritten commit
  instead:
  - `jj edit <rev>`
  - `jj rebase -r <rev> --onto ...`
  - `jj squash --into <rev>`

  Run `jj new` afterward to restore the empty `@` on top.
  Until you do, git/IDE diff views show that commit's
  content as uncommitted ŌĆö alarming-looking but expected.
```

This keeps the "why" the recipe links to (now stated plainly), keeps the fix, drops the "colocated / detaches HEAD / first parent" jargon, and folds in your framing. The merge example sentence ("The most common case is a Merge non-ff recipe close-out ŌĆö a trapezoidal commitŌĆ”") would move out, since it's no longer the lead.

- **Squash is always optional and never foreclosed** ŌĆö you can squash later even after going trapezoidal, if you change your mind.

Decide the shape and get user approval before pushing ŌĆö
the choice is hard to undo once on the target. Surface
the options, wait for the user.

content.text:
Here's where I land, and the one real tension to resolve.

**Current intro:**
> Decide the shape and get user approval before pushing ŌĆö the choice is hard to undo once on the target. Surface the options, wait for the user.

**The tension:** "hard to undo once on the target" sits awkwardly against your "you can always squash later." Both are true but they're pulling opposite ways. I'd reconcile by being precise: once published, changing shape is a **remote rewrite** (force-push, needs approval) ŌĆö that's the friction "hard to undo" was gesturing at ŌĆö but it *is* available, and squash specifically is the always-open escape hatch.

**Proposed rewrite** (intro + mark the default on the bullet + a closing line):

> At close-out the cycle's *content* is fixed; its *published shape* is a separate choice made at push time. Surface the options and get user approval before pushing ŌĆö once on the target, changing shape is a remote rewrite (force-push, needs approval), so choose deliberately.

Then on the bullets, tag the default:
> - **Merge non-ff** *(current default)* ŌĆö `main` gains a merge commit ŌĆ”

And a closing line after the bullets:
> Squash is never foreclosed: a published linear or trapezoidal series can be squashed down later as a remote rewrite if you change your mind.

Three things I want your read on:

1. **Default placement** ŌĆö tag `(current default)` on the Merge non-ff bullet (scannable), or state it in the intro? I lean bullet-tag.
2. **"Squash never foreclosed" line** ŌĆö keep it as a distinct closing sentence, or is that over-explaining? You raised it, so I assume you want it explicit.
3. **The `content fixed / shape chosen at push` framing** ŌĆö does that match how you think about it, or is "content" the wrong word (since squash *does* change commit boundaries)? Maybe "the cycle's *commits* are fixed" is wrong too for the same reason. I might say "the cycle's *work* is done; its *published shape* is the remaining choice."

What's your call on those?

message.content: 1. bullet-tag, 2. remove it, 3. use "work is done"

the choice is hard to undo once on the target. Surface
the options, wait for the user.

- **Squash to one commit** ŌĆö single entry on the target.
  Right for straightforward changes where the Work-N is
  focused on one or two files.
- **Merge non-ff** ŌĆö `main` gains a merge commit
  (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via two
  parents. `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` shows the trapezoidal
  shape. See [Merge non-ff recipe](#merge-non-ff-recipe)
  for the full setup sequence.

  the rewritten commit rather than on an empty change above:

input.new_string:
- **Post-amend `jj new`** ŌĆö jj's working copy is always an
  empty `@` on top of the tree. Commands that rewrite a
  commit in place leave `@` *on* the rewritten commit
  instead:
  - `jj edit <rev>`
  - `jj rebase -r <rev> --onto ...`
  - `jj squash --into <rev>`

  Run `jj new` afterward to restore the empty `@` on top.
  Until you do, git/IDE diff views show that commit's
  content as uncommitted ŌĆö alarming-looking but expected.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Chores-13

Continuation of `chores-12.md` (closed at `0.62.x`). This
file covers the `0.63.0` cycle onward.

Subsection headers that record a release use the
trailing-version format from AGENTS.md: `## Description (X.Y.Z)`.
Reference numbering is file-local ŌĆö see
[`AGENTS.md`](../../AGENTS.md#reference-numbering); chores-13
starts at `[1]`.

## docs: adopt AGENTS.md (0.63.0)

Commits: [[1]]

The bot-instructions file is renamed `CLAUDE.md` ŌåÆ `AGENTS.md`
so Zed and the broader agent-tooling ecosystem (which now
default to `AGENTS.md`) read it natively. Claude Code still
auto-loads only `CLAUDE.md`, so a one-line `CLAUDE.md` import
shim (`@AGENTS.md`) keeps it working ŌĆö both tools read one
canonical file.

- **Single canonical file** ŌĆö `AGENTS.md` holds the content;
  `CLAUDE.md` is a one-line `@AGENTS.md` import for Claude
  Code's loader. Nothing human-facing points at the shim.
- **Live references repointed** ŌĆö every `CLAUDE.md` mention
  and `#anchor` link in live docs (`README.md`,
  `ARCHITECTURE.md`, `notes/`, the `src/push.rs` pre-commit
  doc comment, and `AGENTS.md` itself) now names `AGENTS.md`,
  so links resolve in editors and on GitHub.
- **History left as written** ŌĆö `CLAUDE.md` prose in
  `chores-01..12` and `done.md` records what was true at the
  time and is untouched; only the live navigational anchor
  links in the `chores-10/11/12` headers
  (`#reference-numbering`) were repointed so they don't
  dangle against the one-line `CLAUDE.md` shim.

### Why an import shim, not a symlink

A symlink `CLAUDE.md ŌåÆ AGENTS.md` also satisfies Claude
Code's loader, but the `@AGENTS.md` import was chosen
because it is explicit and visible in a diff and does not
depend on symlink semantics (git symlink handling,
cross-platform checkout). Separately, GitHub renders a
symlinked `.md` as a stub page rather than the target's
markdown, so a `CLAUDE.md#anchor` link through a symlink
would not resolve ŌĆö moot here since live links now point
straight at `AGENTS.md`, but it reinforces keeping
`CLAUDE.md` a real file.

## docs: tighten after-finalize rule (0.63.1)

Commits: [[2]]

The stop-and-wait rule in `cycle-protocol.md` was titled
`### After finalize: stop and wait` and phrased as "final
words go in the approval prompt *before* executing finalize"
ŌĆö wording that assumes finalize is a separate,
manually-invoked step and names only finalize as the
trigger. With `vc-x1 push` the push and `vc-x1 finalize` on
`.claude` are bundled as the tail stages, so there is no
"before finalize" gap left to speak in once the push is
invoked. After `0.63.0`'s push the bot emitted a closing
summary *after* `vc-x1 push` returned, violating the rule
(and likely riding into the `.claude` commit via the
`--delay 10` window).

- **Renamed to "After push or finalize"** ŌĆö both triggers
  are named, in the title and the paragraph: the remote
  crossing is the push itself, and `vc-x1 finalize` on
  `.claude` is the detached step. This also covers a manual
  `jj git push` (push, no finalize) and a manual
  `vc-x1 finalize` (no wrapper).
- **Wrapper case spelled out** ŌĆö `vc-x1 push` does both as
  tail stages (`push-app`, then `vc-x1 finalize` on
  `.claude`): closing words go *before* invoking the wrapper,
  and the bot does not purposely emit anything after it
  returns. The rule names the `vc-x1 finalize` operation, not
  the wrapper's internal `finalize-claude` stage label.

The bot thinks the original lapse was a compliance slip (the
rule was read and quoted, then broken), not a comprehension
gap; the doc change closes the one genuine ambiguity (the
wrapper bundles push + finalize, leaving no gap to speak in)
rather than expecting prose alone to prevent the slip. The
rule lives only in `cycle-protocol.md`; AGENTS.md already
mandates reading it before commit work, so no duplicate
restatement was added there.

## docs: codify merge-non-ff recipe (0.64.0)

Multi-commit cycles default to the merge-non-ff trapezoid,
but the recipe is split between `cycle-protocol.md` and
AGENTS.md with wrinkles re-derived each time (exercised
manually on `0.62.0`). Codify the full recipe and
standardize the flag spelling. Split out of Idea #1 (the
broader cycle-protocol.md codification).

- Recipe: `jj rebase -r <closeout> --onto <prev-closeout>
  --onto <work-tip>` (first `--onto` = trunk ŌåÆ first
  parent), then `jj new <merge>` to lift `@` above the
  merge, then `jj git push --bookmark main`.
- Standardize on `--onto` / `-o` (canonical), not the
  `-d` alias ŌĆö update AGENTS.md's `-d` spellings (jj
  Basics + the post-amend `jj new` note) to `--onto`.
- `@` reverts to the work-tip's content until the
  `jj new` (the "post-amend `jj new`" gotcha already in
  AGENTS.md).
- Post-hoc caveat: if the cycle was already pushed
  keep-separate (as `0.62.0` was), the rebase needs
  `--ignore-immutable` and the push is a force-update of
  `main`; the standard recipe assumes the merge is set up
  before the close-out push.

### As-built ladder

- 0.64.0-0 Preparation ŌĆö version bump, 0.63.1 `Commits:`
  backfill, item pickup. Also clarified the Preparation
  step itself: a Cargo.lock-update bullet, reworded the
  Todo "move into In Progress", and a Prose-form intro
  line at the top of `cycle-protocol.md`.
- 0.64.0-1 cycle-protocol.md ŌĆö promote the recipe to a
  `### Merge non-ff recipe` subsection (rebase ŌåÆ `jj new`
  lift ŌåÆ push, with the parents broken out as sub-bullets
  and a post-hoc caveat); trim the shape bullet to point
  at it.
- 0.64.0-2 AGENTS.md ŌĆö standardize `-d` ŌåÆ `--onto` in the
  post-amend note; add a jj Basics bullet for the
  `--onto`/`-o` spelling; sub-bullet the post-amend
  command list; replace the duplicated merge example with
  a link to the new recipe subsection (no cross-file
  duplication).

file.content:
**docs: codify merge-non-ff recipe (0.64.0)**

Multi-commit cycles default to the merge-non-ff trapezoid,
but the recipe is split between `cycle-protocol.md` and
AGENTS.md with wrinkles re-derived each time (exercised
manually on `0.62.0`). Codify the full recipe and
standardize the flag spelling. Split out of Idea #1 (the
broader cycle-protocol.md codification).

- Recipe: `jj rebase -r <closeout> --onto <prev-closeout>
  --onto <work-tip>` (first `--onto` = trunk ŌåÆ first
  parent), then `jj new <merge>` to lift `@` above the
  merge, then `jj git push --bookmark main`.
- Standardize on `--onto` / `-o` (canonical), not the
  `-d` alias ŌĆö update AGENTS.md's `-d` spellings (jj
  Basics + the post-amend `jj new` note) to `--onto`,
  plus a one-line "`-d` is an alias of `--onto`" so the
  docs don't read as two different flags.
- `@` reverts to the work-tip's content until the
  `jj new` (the "post-amend `jj new`" gotcha already in
  AGENTS.md).
- Post-hoc caveat: if the cycle was already pushed
  keep-separate (as `0.62.0` was), the rebase needs
  `--ignore-immutable` and the push is a force-update of
  `main`; the standard recipe assumes the merge is set up
  before the close-out push.

Plan ladder:

- 0.64.0-0 Preparation (done)
- 0.64.0-1 cycle-protocol.md: promote the recipe to a
  `### Merge non-ff recipe` subsection (rebase ŌåÆ `jj new`
  lift ŌåÆ push, plus the `--ignore-immutable` post-hoc
  caveat); trim the shape bullet to point at it. (done)
- 0.64.0-2 AGENTS.md: standardize `-d` ŌåÆ `--onto` in the
  post-amend note; add a one-line "`-d` is an alias of
  `--onto`". (done)
- 0.64.0 close-out and validation.

s=s[:start]+'_No cycle currently in progress._'+s[end:]
open(p,'w').write(s)
print("done")
EOF

97	## jj Basics
98	
99	- `jj st -R .` / `jj st -R .claude` ŌĆö show working copy status
100	- `jj log -R .` / `jj log -R .claude` ŌĆö show commit log
101	- `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` ŌĆö finalize working copy into a commit
102	- `jj describe -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` ŌĆö set description without committing

file.content:
## jj Basics

- `jj st -R .` / `jj st -R .claude` ŌĆö show working copy status
- `jj log -R .` / `jj log -R .claude` ŌĆö show commit log
- `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` ŌĆö finalize working copy into a commit
- `jj describe -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` ŌĆö set description without committing
- `jj git push --bookmark <name> -R <repo>` ŌĆö push a bookmark (no
  `--allow-new` flag; jj pushes new bookmarks without special flags)
- In jj, the working copy (@) is always a mutable commit being edited.
  `jj commit` finalizes it and creates a new empty working copy on top.
- The `.claude` repo always has uncommitted changes during an active
  session because session data updates continuously.
- `jj rebase` uses `--onto`/`-o` to name the destination(s).
- **Post-amend `jj new`** ŌĆö jj's working copy is always an
  empty `@` on top of the tree. Commands that rewrite a
  commit in place leave `@` *on* the rewritten commit
  instead:
  - `jj edit <rev>`
  - `jj rebase -r <rev> --onto ...`
  - `jj squash --into <rev>`

  Run `jj new` afterward to restore the empty `@` on top.
  Until you do, git/IDE diff views show that commit's
  content as uncommitted ŌĆö alarming-looking but expected.

content.text:
Read it. My honest thought: I'd **not** do the simple-pointer here ŌĆö it reintroduces exactly the circle we just removed, and it costs generality. But I agree lines 109ŌĆō120 read awkwardly, and there's a cleaner fix. Here's the reasoning:

**Why a pointer-to-recipe doesn't work:**
- The post-amend note is **general** ŌĆö it fires for `jj edit`, `jj squash --into`, and any `jj describe`/rebase that rewrites in place, not just the merge close-out. We hit it several times *this session* on squashes. The merge recipe is one instance; pointing only at it loses the other cases.
- **Direction of reference.** Right now the recipe (step 2) says *"See post-amend `jj new` for why."* The "why" (empty-`@` + IDE-shows-uncommitted) lives **only** here. If AGENTS instead points *to* the recipe, the recipe is now the holder of a "why" it doesn't actually contain ŌåÆ dangling, and if we add it there, AGENTSŌåörecipe is circular again.

**What actually reads badly** (and is worth fixing ŌĆö but as wording, not a pointer):
1. **Line 109 is orphaned** ŌĆö a lone `--onto`/`-o` one-liner floating between two unrelated bullets. It'd read better folded into the rebase mention inside the post-amend note (the only place rebase syntax appears).
2. **Redundant opener.** Lines 105ŌĆō106 *already* say "the working copy (@) is always a mutable commit ŌĆ” creates a new empty working copy on top." The post-amend note then re-states "jj's working copy is always an empty `@` on top of the tree." Two bullets establishing the same fact.

So my recommendation: keep the note **general and canonical**, but tighten it ŌĆö drop the redundant opener (105ŌĆō106 already owns that fact), fold line 109's `--onto` into the rebase line, and lead straight with the rewrite-in-place behavior + the `jj new` fix. That fixes the readability complaint without the circle or the generality loss.

Your call on direction ŌĆö want me to draft that tightened version, or do you still prefer the pointer and want to talk through where the "why" would then live?

Good challenges ŌĆö let me not guess on jj semantics. We have direct evidence for one of these from *this very session*, and I'll test the other in a scratch repo.

**`jj squash --into <rev>`** ŌĆö you're right, it doesn't belong. We ran it twice this session (folding the doc edits) and both times `@` *stayed* as the empty working copy on top ŌĆö it didn't land on the rewritten commit:

> `jj squash --into ulpxvpus ŌĆ”` ŌåÆ `Working copy (@) now at: srsusvqx 071c531a (no description set)`

`@` remained `srsusvqx` (empty), just rebased. Squash moves the diff *out of* `@` *into* `<rev>`; `@` is left clean above. No `jj new` needed. So it's miscategorized. (And yes ŌĆö it's `--into`, unrelated to rebase's `--onto`.)

**`jj edit <rev>`** ŌĆö let me verify rather than assert:

`jj edit <rev>` confirmed: after it, `@` **is** that commit (`onqywnkt A`) and `jj st` shows its content as working-copy changes ŌĆö so `@` does land on it and reads "dirty." *But* that's the **intended** state: you ran `jj edit` precisely to sit on that commit and edit it; you `jj new` when you're *done*, not as an immediate "restore." So it doesn't fit the note's "run `jj new` afterward to restore" framing either.

content.text:
Both your instincts check out, and I owe you a correction: this undercuts the "keep it general" argument I made earlier.

**Findings:**
- **`jj squash --into <rev>`** ŌĆö mis-listed. `@` stays clean on top (confirmed twice this session). Remove it.
- **`jj edit <rev>`** ŌĆö lands `@` on the commit, but *deliberately* (you're there to edit); the `jj new` comes when you're *done*, not as a surprise-restore. Doesn't fit the note's "run `jj new` afterward to restore" framing.
- **`jj rebase -r <wc-commit> --onto ŌĆ”`** ŌĆö the one genuine fit (rewrites the working-copy commit into a content-bearing merge, leaving `@` on it, reading dirty). My scratch repro of the two-`--onto` merge errored out, so I won't assert its exact mechanics here ŌĆö but this is the case the note is really about, and it *is* the merge-non-ff recipe.

**So my earlier reasoning was partly wrong.** I argued the note earns its keep by being *general* across edit/rebase/squash. The evidence says it isn't general ŌĆö two of the three don't fit. The accurate residue is essentially the **merge-recipe gotcha**. That makes your original instinct ("just point to `cycle-protocol#merge-non-ff-recipe`") the better call after all.

**The one design care** (so we don't recreate the circle): if AGENTS points *to* the recipe, the recipe must **own the why** and not link back. Concretely:
- Recipe step 2 expands to own it: *"ŌĆ”`jj new` adds an empty `@` above the merge ŌĆö the rebase left `@` on the now-content-bearing merge, which git/IDE show as uncommitted; `jj new` restores a clean `@` on top."* (drop its "See AGENTS for why" link)
- AGENTS jj Basics: keep the `--onto`/`-o` one-liner (generally useful), and **drop the post-amend note** (or shrink to a one-line breadcrumb pointing at the recipe). In practice we never hit the dirty-`@` surprise this session outside the merge case, so its general value is low.

That's a one-directional ref (AGENTS ŌåÆ recipe), no circle, and kills the awkward lines 109ŌĆō120.

No changes made. Want me to go that route ŌĆö and if so, drop the AGENTS post-amend note entirely, or leave a one-line breadcrumb?

Work commit:

1. **Rebase the close-out into a merge** ŌĆö `jj rebase -r
   <closeout> --onto <prev> --onto <work-tip>`.
   - `-r <closeout>` keeps the `<closeout>` commit in place.
   - `--onto <prev>` becomes its first parent (trunk).
   - `--onto <work-tip>` becomes the second parent.

   Together these make `<closeout>` a merge of
   `<prev>` + `<work-tip>`, forming a trapezoidal commit.
2. **Use `jj new <merge>`** to add an empty `@` above the
   merge. The rebase left `@` *on* the now-content-bearing
   merge, which git/IDE diff views show as uncommitted;
   `jj new` restores the clean empty `@` on top.
3. **Push** ŌĆö `jj git push --bookmark main -R .`.

content.text:
I agree with you, and there's a sharper reason than just "tricky" ŌĆö folding would force us to *lie in the `-2` body*.

Here's the chronology that actually happened:
- `0.64.0-2` genuinely **reframed** the post-amend note (empty-`@` framing). That commit's body says so, truthfully.
- *Then*, during review, we decided to **drop** the note entirely and move the "why" into the recipe.

If I fold the drop back into `-2`, I have to rewrite `-2`'s body to pretend it *never* reframed the note ŌĆö erasing a real step. That's the "faking" you're pointing at. A `-3` records the truth: `-2` reframed, `-3` removed. The bodies stay honest and nothing gets reached-back-and-rewritten.

So ranking the three options:

1. **`-3` (your lean)** ŌĆö best. Honest append-only record, no descendant rebases, no body-rewriting of `-1`/`-2`. One `-3` commit ("apply review refinements") touching both cycle-protocol.md (self-contained step 2) and AGENTS.md (drop the note). It mixes two files, but that's a coherent "review pass" unit and the body is file-by-file anyway. Chores As-built just gains a `-3` rung.
2. **Let it ride in close-out (your "doesn't have to be minimal")** ŌĆö works, but I'd resist: it breaks the "close-out = bookkeeping only" invariant, so a reader who opens the `0.64.0` commit expecting bookkeeping finds a recipe rewrite. The blast radius is one cycle, but the invariant is cheap to keep.
3. **Fold into `-1`/`-2`** ŌĆö the one I proposed, now my least-favorite for the reason above.

On the "ladder is scratch" angle: since the *published* shape is chosen at push (squash/merge/keep), keeping `-3` separate costs nothing downstream ŌĆö if we squash, it all collapses anyway; if we merge/keep, the `-3` honestly shows the review round.

Mechanics note (no changes yet): `@` is mixed, so going `-3` means extracting the two doc edits from `@` into a new `0.64.0-3` commit (bump Cargo to `-3`), leaving chores/todo + Cargo-`0.64.0` for the close-out. The cargo cycle we already ran still stands (docs-only). Ordering's fine since close-out is uncommitted.

My vote: **`-3`**. Want to go that way?

lastPrompt: My reaction is that is too tricky "close-out" and doesn't have to be minimal, IMO. If we really want to separate these changes from the close-out I'd lean towards a -3, that records what we did ratherŌĆ”

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Chores-13

Continuation of `chores-12.md` (closed at `0.62.x`). This
file covers the `0.63.0` cycle onward.

Subsection headers that record a release use the
trailing-version format from AGENTS.md: `## Description (X.Y.Z)`.
Reference numbering is file-local ŌĆö see
[`AGENTS.md`](../../AGENTS.md#reference-numbering); chores-13
starts at `[1]`.

## docs: adopt AGENTS.md (0.63.0)

Commits: [[1]]

The bot-instructions file is renamed `CLAUDE.md` ŌåÆ `AGENTS.md`
so Zed and the broader agent-tooling ecosystem (which now
default to `AGENTS.md`) read it natively. Claude Code still
auto-loads only `CLAUDE.md`, so a one-line `CLAUDE.md` import
shim (`@AGENTS.md`) keeps it working ŌĆö both tools read one
canonical file.

- **Single canonical file** ŌĆö `AGENTS.md` holds the content;
  `CLAUDE.md` is a one-line `@AGENTS.md` import for Claude
  Code's loader. Nothing human-facing points at the shim.
- **Live references repointed** ŌĆö every `CLAUDE.md` mention
  and `#anchor` link in live docs (`README.md`,
  `ARCHITECTURE.md`, `notes/`, the `src/push.rs` pre-commit
  doc comment, and `AGENTS.md` itself) now names `AGENTS.md`,
  so links resolve in editors and on GitHub.
- **History left as written** ŌĆö `CLAUDE.md` prose in
  `chores-01..12` and `done.md` records what was true at the
  time and is untouched; only the live navigational anchor
  links in the `chores-10/11/12` headers
  (`#reference-numbering`) were repointed so they don't
  dangle against the one-line `CLAUDE.md` shim.

### Why an import shim, not a symlink

A symlink `CLAUDE.md ŌåÆ AGENTS.md` also satisfies Claude
Code's loader, but the `@AGENTS.md` import was chosen
because it is explicit and visible in a diff and does not
depend on symlink semantics (git symlink handling,
cross-platform checkout). Separately, GitHub renders a
symlinked `.md` as a stub page rather than the target's
markdown, so a `CLAUDE.md#anchor` link through a symlink
would not resolve ŌĆö moot here since live links now point
straight at `AGENTS.md`, but it reinforces keeping
`CLAUDE.md` a real file.

## docs: tighten after-finalize rule (0.63.1)

Commits: [[2]]

The stop-and-wait rule in `cycle-protocol.md` was titled
`### After finalize: stop and wait` and phrased as "final
words go in the approval prompt *before* executing finalize"
ŌĆö wording that assumes finalize is a separate,
manually-invoked step and names only finalize as the
trigger. With `vc-x1 push` the push and `vc-x1 finalize` on
`.claude` are bundled as the tail stages, so there is no
"before finalize" gap left to speak in once the push is
invoked. After `0.63.0`'s push the bot emitted a closing
summary *after* `vc-x1 push` returned, violating the rule
(and likely riding into the `.claude` commit via the
`--delay 10` window).

- **Renamed to "After push or finalize"** ŌĆö both triggers
  are named, in the title and the paragraph: the remote
  crossing is the push itself, and `vc-x1 finalize` on
  `.claude` is the detached step. This also covers a manual
  `jj git push` (push, no finalize) and a manual
  `vc-x1 finalize` (no wrapper).
- **Wrapper case spelled out** ŌĆö `vc-x1 push` does both as
  tail stages (`push-app`, then `vc-x1 finalize` on
  `.claude`): closing words go *before* invoking the wrapper,
  and the bot does not purposely emit anything after it
  returns. The rule names the `vc-x1 finalize` operation, not
  the wrapper's internal `finalize-claude` stage label.

The bot thinks the original lapse was a compliance slip (the
rule was read and quoted, then broken), not a comprehension
gap; the doc change closes the one genuine ambiguity (the
wrapper bundles push + finalize, leaving no gap to speak in)
rather than expecting prose alone to prevent the slip. The
rule lives only in `cycle-protocol.md`; AGENTS.md already
mandates reading it before commit work, so no duplicate
restatement was added there.

## docs: codify merge-non-ff recipe (0.64.0)

Multi-commit cycles default to the merge-non-ff trapezoid,
but the recipe is split between `cycle-protocol.md` and
AGENTS.md with wrinkles re-derived each time (exercised
manually on `0.62.0`). Codify the full recipe and
standardize the flag spelling. Split out of Idea #1 (the
broader cycle-protocol.md codification).

- Recipe: `jj rebase -r <closeout> --onto <prev-closeout>
  --onto <work-tip>` (first `--onto` = trunk ŌåÆ first
  parent), then `jj new <merge>` to lift `@` above the
  merge, then `jj git push --bookmark main`.
- Standardize on `--onto` / `-o` (canonical), not the
  `-d` alias ŌĆö update AGENTS.md's `-d` spellings (jj
  Basics + the post-amend `jj new` note) to `--onto`.
- `@` reverts to the work-tip's content until the
  `jj new` (the "post-amend `jj new`" gotcha already in
  AGENTS.md).
- Post-hoc caveat: if the cycle was already pushed
  keep-separate (as `0.62.0` was), the rebase needs
  `--ignore-immutable` and the push is a force-update of
  `main`; the standard recipe assumes the merge is set up
  before the close-out push.

### As-built ladder

- 0.64.0-0 Preparation ŌĆö version bump, 0.63.1 `Commits:`

content.text:
The cycle is complete locally: `-0 ŌåÆ -1 ŌåÆ -2 ŌåÆ -3 ŌåÆ 0.64.0`, nothing pushed. Now the **shape decision** ŌĆö how this lands on `main`. Three options:

1. **Merge non-ff** *(the current default ŌĆö and we'd dogfood the recipe we just codified)* ŌĆö a trapezoidal `0.64.0` merge commit; all five cycle commits stay reachable. Setup per the new recipe: `jj rebase -r olyomswu --onto tplqwyry --onto srsusvqx` ŌåÆ `jj new <merge>` ŌåÆ push. Honest test of the doc we just wrote.
2. **Squash to one commit** ŌĆö a single `0.64.0` on `main`. Cleanest history, but collapses the `-3` review round we deliberately kept separate for honesty.
3. **Keep separate** ŌĆö all five commits linear on `main`. Fully supported by `vc-x1 push`; the other two need manual jj steps.

One caveat for the merge route: `vc-x1 push` only fully supports keep-separate, and merge-non-ff also needs the N:1 codeŌåöbot `ochid` handling (still an open Todo), so it'd be hand-driven jj ŌĆö a good stress test but more manual.

My lean: **merge non-ff**, to dogfood what this cycle produced ŌĆö but it's your call, and it's the more manual path. Which shape, and is the push target `main`?

I'll wait for your choice before any `jj rebase`/`jj git push`.
